title: Information Exploration
text:
Preface
This book teaches you how to use information to identify interesting real-world problems and generate insight. You will learn to find, collect, assemble, and organize data to inspire new questions, make predictions, generate deliverables, and work toward solutions. Along the way, you will develop the practical skills of exploratory data analysis using Python and SQL — two tools that appear in virtually every data-oriented career.
If the word “exploratory” feels a little abstract, that is intentional. Real analysis is rarely linear. You load data, discover it is messier than you expected, go back to clean it, realize you need a different angle, and iterate. This book teaches that rhythm — not only the individual techniques, but the habits of mind that turn a pile of rows into a defensible conclusion.

title: Information Exploration
text:
Who This Book Is For
This book is written for undergraduate students who have completed one introductory course in Python. You should be comfortable with variables, loops, conditionals, functions, and lists. If you can write a for loop that iterates over a list and appends results to another list, you have enough Python background to begin.
No prior experience with pandas, SQL, databases, or the command line is required. The book introduces each of these tools from scratch, with careful attention to the concepts that trip up newcomers.

title: Information Exploration
text:
What You Will Learn
By the time you finish this book, you will be able to:

Load tabular data from CSV, Excel, and SQLite sources into a working environment — and recognize the silent failures (wrong headers, misparsed types, hidden footnotes) that happen along the way.
Clean messy real-world data using type coercion, string methods, missing-value handling, and deduplication, and document every decision you make in a Known Issues Log.
Derive new information from existing datasets with grouped aggregation, pivot tables, and multi-table joins, auditing each operation to confirm nothing silently broke.
Translate the same analytical moves between pandas and SQL, so you can choose the right tool for the job regardless of where the data lives.
Characterize distributions, build visualizations with matplotlib and seaborn, and run simple hypothesis tests to answer real questions about public data.
Read Python error messages and tracebacks fluently, turning “walls of red text” into actionable diagnostic information.

You do not need to memorize every function or argument you encounter. Learning to navigate documentation is one of the most valuable skills this book will help you build — and you will come back to the docs for the rest of your career.

title: Information Exploration
text:
The Load–Clean–Extend–Reshape Framework
Data analysis is often taught as a grab bag of techniques. This book takes a different approach: it organizes the entire curriculum around a four-stage workflow that applies regardless of the tool you are using.
Loading is the process of getting data from its source — a CSV file, an Excel spreadsheet, a database — into your working environment. This stage seems simple, but it is where many invisible problems originate: wrong delimiters, misidentified headers, encoding errors, and columns silently cast to the wrong type.
Cleaning addresses the problems that loading reveals. You will learn to identify and fix data type mismatches, standardize inconsistent categories, handle missing values, detect duplicates, and document every decision you make along the way.
Extending means deriving new information from what you already have. This includes computing new columns, building grouped summaries and aggregation tables, and combining information from multiple sources. The goal is to transform raw observations into metrics that can support analysis.
Reshaping is the process of changing the structure of your data — pivoting between wide and long formats, merging tables on shared keys, and auditing the results to confirm nothing was lost or duplicated in the process.
These four stages are not strictly sequential. Real analysis involves cycling back through them as you discover new issues. But the framework gives you a reliable starting point and a vocabulary for describing where you are in any analysis workflow.

title: Information Exploration
text:
How This Book Is Organized
The book is divided into four parts. Parts II and III each apply the Load–Clean–Extend–Reshape framework with a different tool; Part I builds the conceptual and technical foundation; Part IV applies the results to analysis.
Part I: Foundations covers the paratechnical skills that most courses skip but that every analyst needs — navigating the terminal, organizing project files, writing documentation, managing Python environments, and reading error messages — as well as the conceptual and ethical vocabulary for working with public data. Two chapters introduce ideas rather than tools: one on formulating research questions before the technical work begins, and one on the dynamics that shape public datasets and the practices that make data work accountable. These short chapters are designed to be referenced throughout the rest of the book.
Part II: Python introduces the pandas library for tabular data analysis. Across four chapters, you will learn to load CSV and Excel files with read_csv and read_excel, clean data using string methods, type coercion, and dictionary-based recoding, extend datasets with derived columns, groupby aggregation, and pivot_table, and reshape data using melt, merge, and concat. The datasets in this section include Colorado cannabis sales data, Boulder County voter registration records, Lahman Baseball statistics, and Colorado population estimates from the State Demography Office.
Part III: SQL teaches you to perform the same operations using Structured Query Language and the SQLite database engine. You will set up a database from the command line, design schemas, load cleaned CSVs, write diagnostic queries, use GROUP BY, subqueries, and Common Table Expressions (CTEs), and join multiple tables while auditing join quality. The SQL chapters use Colorado public health datasets — CDC mortality data, Colorado Bureau of Investigation crime statistics, and population estimates — culminating in a multi-table join that produces an analysis-ready dataset.
Part IV: Analysis applies the conceptual vocabulary from Part I to the clean, well-structured data you have built. You will characterize distributions and identify outliers through descriptive statistics, build a visual vocabulary for exploring patterns using matplotlib and seaborn, and apply simple hypothesis tests from scipy.stats — t-tests, chi-square tests, and correlation coefficients — interpreting their results in plain language. Part IV uses the same datasets from earlier chapters, so that the analytical questions grow naturally from the data preparation work you have already done.
An Appendix describes each dataset used in the book, including its source, structure, key columns, and known quirks.

title: Information Exploration
text:
Recurring Themes
Several principles recur throughout the book, regardless of whether you are working in pandas or SQL.
Inspect before you act. Every chapter begins by looking at the data before transforming it. The post-load checklist — shape, headers, types, missingness, distributions, duplicates, plausibility, and a quick visual scan — appears in every loading chapter and becomes second nature.
Validate after every transformation. Cleaning and reshaping operations can silently drop rows, create duplicates, or introduce null values. The book teaches you to check row counts, compare totals, and cross-verify results after every step.
Make invisible problems visible. Many data errors are silent — a join that drops rows because of trailing whitespace in a key column, a column that looks numeric but contains a hidden sentinel value, a merge that inflates row counts because of an unexpected many-to-many relationship. A central goal of this book is to train your eye to catch these problems before they corrupt your analysis.
Document your decisions. Every cleaning choice involves a judgment call. The book introduces the Known Issues Log and Data Dictionary as lightweight documentation tools that make your workflow reproducible and your assumptions transparent.

title: Information Exploration
text:
Conventions Used in This Book
Code blocks contain Python or SQL that you can run in a Jupyter notebook or SQLite shell. When a code example includes output, it appears directly below the code block. Exercises appear throughout each chapter with solutions hidden in collapsible blocks — try the exercise before revealing the answer.
Terminal commands are shown in bash code blocks. On Windows, equivalent commands are noted where they differ.

title: Information Exploration
text:
Accompanying Materials
All datasets used in this book are available in the accompanying GitHub repository. Each chapter assumes you have downloaded the relevant data files to your working directory. Instructions for setting up your environment appear in 4  Python Environments.

title: Information Exploration
text:
A Note on Difficulty
Some chapters will feel harder than others, and that is not a reflection of your ability. Data work is a craft that rewards patience — you will write code that does not work, read error messages that look like nonsense, and spend an hour tracking down a single stray character. This is completely normal. Every experienced data analyst goes through the same cycle, and the difference between a beginner and a professional is not that professionals do not get stuck. It is that they have learned which moves to try first when they do.
If something in this book feels confusing, slow down rather than push through. Re-read the section. Run the code one line at a time. Print the intermediate results. The concepts in this book will click with practice — often on the third or fourth real project, not the first. Stay with it.

title: Information Exploration
text:
Further Reading






Tip📚 General resources that complement this book






Python documentation — the official Python tutorial is an excellent reference when you need to refresh syntax or a standard-library module.
pandas user guide — pandas.pydata.org/docs/user_guide/ is longer than you want it to be but worth bookmarking. The “10 minutes to pandas” page is a useful quick reference.
SQLite documentation — sqlite.org/docs.html includes the full SQL syntax reference. SQLZoo and Mode’s SQL tutorial are both free, interactive, and beginner-friendly.
Real Python — realpython.com publishes high-quality, beginner-friendly tutorials on almost every topic in this book.
“Python for Data Analysis” by Wes McKinney (the creator of pandas) — freely available at wesmckinney.com/book/. A natural next read after this book.

title: Information Exploration
text:
Acknowledgments
This book grew out of the INFO 3401: Information Exploration course at the University of Colorado Boulder. It reflects the contributions of many students whose questions, mistakes, and feedback shaped the material into its current form.

title: 1  The Terminal and File System
text:
1.1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:
crumbs: 1  The Terminal and File System

title: 1  The Terminal and File System
text:
Open a terminal on macOS, Windows, and Linux, and identify the prompt and working directory.
Navigate a file system using pwd, ls, and cd, and distinguish between relative and absolute paths.
Create, move, copy, and inspect files from the command line.
Diagnose command not found errors by reasoning about the system PATH.
crumbs: 1  The Terminal and File System

title: 1  The Terminal and File System
text:
1.2 Why the Terminal Matters
Every graphical application you use — Jupyter Notebook, VS Code, Excel, your file manager — is a visual wrapper around operations that happen in the terminal. When you double-click a file to open it, your operating system is running a command. When you save a file, it is writing bytes to a location on disk. Learning to use the terminal directly gives you access to the same operations without the graphical middleman, which matters for three practical reasons.
First, many data tools — including SQLite, git, and package managers like conda — are designed to be used from the terminal. You will encounter them throughout this book and throughout your career. Second, terminal commands are scriptable: you can record a sequence of steps in a file and replay them exactly, which makes your workflow reproducible. Third, when something goes wrong in a graphical tool, the terminal is often the only way to diagnose the problem.
This chapter introduces the small set of terminal commands you will use repeatedly in this book. You do not need to memorize them — you need to understand what they do so you can look them up when you need them. If the terminal feels intimidating at first, that is completely normal. You are learning a new interface to your own computer, and the fluency comes with repetition.
crumbs: 1  The Terminal and File System

title: 1  The Terminal and File System
text:
1.3 Opening a Terminal
On macOS, open the Terminal application (found in Applications → Utilities, or search for “Terminal” with Spotlight). On Windows, open Anaconda Prompt from the Start menu — this is a terminal preconfigured with access to your Python environment. You can also use PowerShell or Windows Terminal, though some commands differ. On Linux, open your distribution’s terminal emulator.
When the terminal opens, you see a prompt — a line of text ending with $ (Mac/Linux) or > (Windows) — followed by a blinking cursor. This is where you type commands. The terminal executes each command when you press Enter and prints any output below it.
crumbs: 1  The Terminal and File System

title: 1  The Terminal and File System
section: 1.4 Where Am I? Navigating the File System
text:
1.4 Where Am I? Navigating the File System
Your computer’s file system is a tree of directories (folders) and files. At any moment, your terminal session is “inside” one directory, called the working directory. Every command you run operates relative to this location unless you specify an absolute path.

1.4.1 Printing the working directory
To find out where you are, use pwd (print working directory):
pwd
On Mac or Linux, you might see something like /Users/yourname or /home/yourname. On Windows, the equivalent command is cd with no arguments, and the output looks like C:\Users\yourname.


1.4.2 Listing files
To see what is in the current directory, use ls (list):
ls
On Windows, the equivalent is dir. To see more detail — file sizes, modification dates, and hidden files — use ls -la on Mac/Linux. The -l flag means “long format” (one file per line with details) and -a means “all files” (including hidden files whose names start with a dot, like .gitignore).


1.4.3 Changing directories
To move into a subdirectory, use cd (change directory) followed by the folder name:
cd Documents
To move up one level (to the parent directory), use cd ..:
cd ..
To jump to your home directory from anywhere, use cd ~ on Mac/Linux or cd %USERPROFILE% on Windows.


1.4.4 Absolute vs. relative paths
A relative path starts from your current directory: cd Documents/projects moves into the projects folder inside Documents. An absolute path starts from the root of the file system: /Users/yourname/Documents/projects on Mac, or C:\Users\yourname\Documents\projects on Windows. Absolute paths work regardless of where your terminal session currently is.






TipBuilding a mental model



Think of pwd as asking “Where am I?”, ls as asking “What’s here?”, and cd as saying “Go there.” These three commands are the navigation triad you will use constantly.
crumbs: 1  The Terminal and File System

title: 1  The Terminal and File System
text:
1.5 Creating and Managing Files

1.5.1 Creating directories
Use mkdir (make directory) to create a new folder:
mkdir my_project
To create nested directories in one step, add the -p flag:
mkdir -p my_project/data/raw   # -p creates all intermediate directories at once
                               # without -p, mkdir errors if a parent doesn't exist


1.5.2 Moving and copying files
Use cp to copy a file and mv to move (or rename) a file:
cp original.csv backup.csv          # copy
mv old_name.csv new_name.csv        # rename
mv data.csv data/raw/data.csv       # move to a subdirectory
On Windows, the equivalents are copy and move.


1.5.3 Viewing file contents
To see the first few lines of a text file without opening it in an editor, use head:
head -5 my_data.csv
This prints the first 5 lines — useful for checking whether a CSV has the header row you expect. On Windows, use Get-Content my_data.csv -Head 5 in PowerShell, or type my_data.csv | more in Command Prompt.
crumbs: 1  The Terminal and File System

title: 1  The Terminal and File System
text:
1.6 Paths and Your Python Environment
When you run a command like python or sqlite3, your terminal searches for that program in a list of directories called the PATH. If the program is not in any of those directories, you get a command not found error — even if the program is installed somewhere on your computer.
This is the most common source of confusion when setting up tools. The fix is usually one of two things: either install the program through a package manager that automatically updates your PATH (such as conda install sqlite), or manually add the program’s directory to your PATH. The details vary by operating system, but the concept is the same everywhere. Managing Python environments — including configuring the PATH correctly — is covered in Chapter 4.
The directory you navigate to in your terminal is your working directory for any scripts or notebooks you run from that location. Organizing your project files consistently makes this easier to manage; see Chapter 2 for the recommended directory structure.






WarningThe most common Windows mistake



After installing a new tool or editing your PATH, you must open a new terminal window. The old window will not see the change.
crumbs: 1  The Terminal and File System

title: 1  The Terminal and File System
text:
1.7 Common Issues in This Chapter






Warningcommand not found or 'cd' is not recognized



What it means: The shell cannot find the program you typed. Either you are on the wrong operating system’s equivalent command (ls on Windows Command Prompt does not exist — use dir), the program is not installed, or it is installed but not on your PATH.
How to fix it: Confirm you are using the correct command for your OS. If a tool you installed is missing, close and reopen your terminal. If that does not work, re-read the install instructions — most tools print the PATH line you need to add at the end of their installer output.








WarningNo such file or directory



What it means: The file or folder name you typed does not exist relative to your current working directory.
How to fix it: Run pwd (or cd with no arguments on Windows) to confirm where you are. Then run ls (or dir) to see what is actually in the folder. Spelling and capitalization matter — Documents and documents are different on Mac and Linux.








WarningPermission denied



What it means: Your user account does not have the rights to read, write, or execute the target. This is most common when you try to modify system folders or files created by another user.
How to fix it: Avoid using sudo reflexively — stay in your home directory (~ on Mac/Linux, %USERPROFILE% on Windows) for your own projects. If you genuinely need to change a system file’s permissions, learn what you are doing first; breaking system permissions can render your machine unbootable.
crumbs: 1  The Terminal and File System

title: 1  The Terminal and File System
text:
1.8 Key Takeaways

The terminal is a text interface to the same operations your graphical tools perform. It is not advanced or scary — it is a different surface for actions you already know.
pwd, ls, and cd are the navigation triad. Build reflex memory for these three commands before worrying about anything else.
Paths can be relative (from where you are) or absolute (from the root of the file system). Know which you are using.
command not found almost always means the program is not on your PATH, not that it is missing. Open a fresh terminal after any install.
crumbs: 1  The Terminal and File System

1.9 Further Reading






Tip📚 Going deeper on the terminal






The Missing Semester of Your CS Education (MIT) — missing.csail.mit.edu is an excellent free course on the terminal, shell scripting, and developer tooling.
Bash cheat sheet — devhints.io/bash is a compact reference for common shell commands.

title: 1  The Terminal and File System
text:
1.10 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: Navigate to your project folder





Open a terminal and use pwd, ls, and cd to navigate to the folder where you keep your course materials. Create a new subdirectory called data inside it. Verify the directory was created by listing the contents of the parent folder. See Chapter 2 for the recommended directory structure for course projects.









NoteExercise 2: Inspect a CSV from the terminal





Download one of the course datasets to your project folder. Use head (or the Windows equivalent) to view the first 10 lines. Based on what you see, answer: does the file have a single header row? What delimiter does it use? Are there any obvious problems visible in the first few rows?









NoteExercise 3: Exploring the PATH





Run echo $PATH in a terminal (Mac/Linux) or echo %PATH% (Windows). How many directories are listed? Then run which python (Mac/Linux) or where python (Windows) to find where your Python executable lives. Is that directory in your PATH? Close the terminal, open a new one, and run echo $PATH again — does the output match?
Explain in one sentence: what would happen if your Python installation directory were not in the PATH?






NoteSolution





If Python’s directory is not in the PATH, running python from the terminal gives a command not found error even though Python is installed. The operating system searches each directory in PATH in order and stops at the first match. If none of the directories contain the executable, the shell reports it as not found.

title: 2  Project Organization
text:
2.1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:

title: 2  Project Organization
text:
Organize a data project into data/raw, data/clean, notebooks, and output directories that scale from homework to professional work.
Apply machine-friendly naming conventions (lowercase, ISO dates, no spaces) to files and folders.
Write a minimal README.md that tells a future reader what a project is, where its data came from, and how to run it.
Write portable relative paths (../data/raw/...) in notebooks instead of brittle absolute paths.

title: 2  Project Organization
text:
2.2 Why Structure Matters
A well-organized project is one you can return to after six months and understand immediately. It is also one that someone else — a collaborator, an instructor, a future employer reviewing your portfolio — can open and navigate without asking you questions. This chapter introduces a simple project structure that scales from a homework assignment to a professional analysis. The terminal commands from Chapter 1 — mkdir, cd, ls — are how you create and navigate this structure from the command line.

title: 2  Project Organization
text:
2.3 The Problem Good Structure Prevents
When you first start working with data, everything tends to end up in one folder: the raw data files, the cleaned versions, the notebooks, the outputs, and the half-finished experiments. This works until it doesn’t. The moment you have two versions of a dataset and cannot remember which one you cleaned, or you overwrite your raw data with a transformed version, you have a problem that no amount of technical skill can fix.
Good project organization prevents these problems before they arise. It costs almost nothing to set up and saves enormous time later.

title: 2  Project Organization
text:
2.4 A Minimal Project Structure
Here is a structure that works for the projects in this book and adapts easily to larger work:
my_project/
├── data/
│   ├── raw/           # Original, untouched data files
│   └── clean/         # Cleaned versions ready for analysis
├── notebooks/         # Jupyter notebooks (analysis work)
├── output/            # Tables, figures, exported results
└── README.md          # What this project is about
The key principle is the separation between raw and clean data. Raw data files are sacred — you should never modify them directly. Instead, your notebooks read from raw/, perform transformations, and write results to clean/ or output/. If something goes wrong, you can always start over from the original files.

title: 2  Project Organization
text:
2.5 Naming Conventions
File names should be descriptive, consistent, and machine-friendly. A few rules that eliminate most naming headaches:
Use lowercase letters, numbers, hyphens, and underscores. Avoid spaces, special characters, and accented letters. Spaces in file names cause problems in the terminal (you have to escape them with backslashes or wrap the name in quotes), and special characters can break scripts that process files automatically.
Include dates in ISO format (YYYY-MM-DD) when versioning matters. report-2026-02-10.csv sorts chronologically; report-Feb-10.csv does not.
Be specific. data.csv tells you nothing. colorado-cannabis-sales-by-county-2014-2024.csv tells you exactly what is inside without opening the file.

title: 2  Project Organization
text:
2.6 The README File
Every project should have a README.md file in its root directory. This file answers three questions: what is this project about, what data does it use, and how do you run it. Writing README files and keeping a Known Issues Log are part of the broader documentation practices described in Chapter 3. A minimal README for a course project might look like this:
# Cannabis Sales Analysis

Analysis of Colorado cannabis sales by county (2014–2024) for INFO 3401.

## Data

- `data/raw/colorado_cannabis_sales_by_county.csv`: Source file from
  Colorado Department of Revenue. Downloaded 2026-02-10.

## Notebooks

- `notebooks/01-loading.ipynb`: Load and inspect raw data.
- `notebooks/02-cleaning.ipynb`: Clean column names, fix types, remove totals.
Writing this file takes five minutes and makes your project immediately legible to anyone who opens it.

title: 2  Project Organization
section: 2.7 Working Directories and File Paths in Notebooks
text:
2.7 Working Directories and File Paths in Notebooks
When you open a Jupyter notebook, it runs in the directory where the notebook file lives. Your Python environment — and whether commands like python and jupyter are available from the terminal — must be configured correctly before notebooks will run; see Chapter 4 for setup instructions. If your notebook is in my_project/notebooks/ and your data is in my_project/data/raw/, you need a relative path that goes up one level and then down into data:
df = pd.read_csv('../data/raw/cannabis_sales.csv')
The .. means “go up one directory.” This relative path works on any computer with the same project structure, regardless of where the project folder itself lives. Avoid hardcoding absolute paths like /Users/yourname/Documents/my_project/data/raw/cannabis_sales.csv — they break the moment someone else tries to run your notebook.






TipA common frustration



If pd.read_csv() gives you a FileNotFoundError, the problem is almost always that your notebook’s working directory is not where you think it is. Run import os; print(os.getcwd()) to check, then adjust your path accordingly.

title: 2  Project Organization
text:
2.8 Version Control (A Brief Preview)
As your projects grow, you will benefit from a version control system like git, which tracks every change you make to every file and lets you revert to any previous state. Git is beyond the scope of this book, but knowing it exists shapes how you organize your work. The project structure described above — with raw data separated from outputs, and a README explaining the project — is designed to work naturally with version control.
If you want to explore git, GitHub’s Git Handbook and the free Pro Git book are excellent starting points. For now, the discipline of keeping raw data untouched and documenting your workflow in notebooks gives you many of the same benefits.

title: 2  Project Organization
text:
2.9 Common Issues in This Chapter






WarningFileNotFoundError in a notebook that used to work



What it means: Your notebook’s working directory changed, or the file moved. The relative path you wrote no longer resolves.
How to fix it: Run import os; print(os.getcwd()) in the notebook. Confirm the notebook is running from where you expect. If the working directory is wrong, either move the notebook back into notebooks/ or update the relative path.








WarningYou accidentally overwrote a raw data file



What it means: A cell that was meant to save a cleaned version wrote back to the raw/ file, and the original is lost.
How to fix it: This is why raw data stays read-only. Re-download from the original source. From now on, always save cleaned outputs to data/clean/ (or output/), and never reuse the same variable name for input and output files.








WarningTwo datasets have the same short name



What it means: You downloaded data.csv from two different sources and one overwrote the other, or you cannot remember which is which.
How to fix it: Rename both with descriptive, dated names (cannabis-sales-2024.csv, voter-registration-2024.csv) and add a line in your README explaining each source. Treat the original filename from the publisher as a starting point, not a final answer.

title: 2  Project Organization
text:
2.10 Key Takeaways

Separate raw/ from clean/ and never modify raw data in place. Treat the raw files as evidence.
Descriptive, machine-friendly filenames (lowercase, ISO dates, no spaces) prevent small frustrations from compounding into lost time.
A five-minute README makes your project legible to a future version of yourself and anyone else who opens it.
Use relative paths (../data/raw/file.csv) so your notebook runs on any computer with the same structure.

title: 2  Project Organization
text:
2.11 Further Reading






Tip📚 Project organization and reproducible analysis






Cookiecutter Data Science — drivendata.github.io/cookiecutter-data-science/ is a widely-used project template that scales up from the structure in this chapter.
Good Enough Practices in Scientific Computing — plos.org is a short paper describing minimum viable reproducibility practices. Worth reading.
GitHub Docs: using Git — docs.github.com/get-started/using-git is the friendliest gateway into version control.
Markdown Guide — markdownguide.org documents the syntax for README.md files.

title: 2  Project Organization
text:
2.12 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: Set up a project





Create a project directory for this course using the structure described above, using the mkdir and cd commands from Chapter 1. Add a README.md that describes the project. Download one of the course datasets into data/raw/. Create a notebook in notebooks/ that loads the dataset using a relative path and prints its shape.









NoteExercise 2: Naming audit





Look at the files on your computer’s desktop or downloads folder. Identify three file names that violate the naming conventions described in this chapter. For each, write a better name and explain why it is an improvement.

title: 3  Documentation and Markdown
text:
3.1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:

title: 3  Documentation and Markdown
text:
Write basic Markdown — headings, emphasis, lists, links, code blocks, and tables — in notebooks, README files, and GitHub.
Structure a notebook with alternating narrative Markdown and code cells using the goal/method/interpretation pattern.
Maintain a Known Issues Log that records each cleaning decision with problem, fix, impact, and remaining uncertainty.
Build a Data Dictionary that describes every column, its type, its valid values, and the cleaning applied.
Write code comments that explain why rather than what.

title: 3  Documentation and Markdown
text:
3.2 Why Documentation Matters
The most technically brilliant analysis is worthless if no one — including your future self — can understand what it does or why it does it. Documentation is not an afterthought you add when the work is finished; it is a running record you maintain while the work is happening. This chapter covers the tools and habits that make documentation practical rather than burdensome.

title: 3  Documentation and Markdown
section: 3.3 Why You Document As You Go
text:
3.3 Why You Document As You Go
There is a persistent fantasy in data work that you will finish the analysis first and document it later. In practice, “later” never comes, and even when it does, you have forgotten the reasoning behind your decisions. Why did you drop those 23 rows? Why did you recode “OTH” as missing instead of keeping it? Why did you use a left join instead of an inner join?
These decisions are judgment calls, not mechanical steps. If you do not record them at the time you make them, they are lost. The solution is to build documentation into your workflow so that it requires almost no extra effort.

title: 3  Documentation and Markdown
text:
3.4 Markdown Basics
Markdown is a lightweight formatting language used in Jupyter notebooks, README files, GitHub, and many other tools. You write plain text with a few simple markers, and the rendering engine converts it into formatted output.

3.4.1 Headings
Use # symbols to create headings. More # symbols mean smaller headings:
# Top-Level Heading
## Section Heading
### Subsection Heading


3.4.2 Emphasis
Wrap text in asterisks for emphasis:
*italic text*
**bold text**
`inline code`


3.4.3 Lists
Create unordered lists with dashes or asterisks, and ordered lists with numbers:
- First item
- Second item
- Third item

1. Step one
2. Step two
3. Step three


3.4.4 Links
[Link text](https://example.com)


3.4.5 Code blocks
For multi-line code, use triple backticks with an optional language identifier:
```python
df = pd.read_csv('data.csv')
df.head()
```


3.4.6 Tables
| Column | Type | Description |
|--------|------|-------------|
| county | str  | County name |
| year   | int  | Calendar year |
These few elements cover the vast majority of what you need for data documentation. You do not need to learn the full Markdown specification — these basics are enough.

title: 3  Documentation and Markdown
text:
3.5 Documentation in Jupyter Notebooks
A Jupyter notebook interleaves Markdown cells with code cells. This makes it the ideal environment for literate analysis, where your explanations sit directly alongside the code they describe.

3.5.1 Narrative Markdown cells
Before each code section, add a Markdown cell that explains what you are about to do and why. After a code section that produces output, add a cell that interprets the result. This narrative structure turns a notebook from a collection of code snippets into a readable analysis.
A useful pattern is to frame each section with three elements: the goal (what question are you answering?), the method (what operation are you performing?), and the interpretation (what did you learn from the result?).


3.5.2 The Known Issues Log
One of the most valuable documentation habits is maintaining a running log of problems you discover and decisions you make during cleaning. The Known Issues Log is a Markdown cell — typically placed near the top or bottom of your notebook — that records each issue with a consistent structure:
## Known Issues and Cleaning Decisions

### 2026-02-17: Party field standardization
**Problem:** Party stored inconsistently — "Dem", "DEM", "D", "Democrat"
all mean the same thing.
**Fix:** Created party_map dictionary, normalized to "DEM", "REP", "UNA".
**Impact:** Reduced unique values from 47 to 3; no rows lost.
**Remaining issue:** 23 rows have party="OTH" — unclear what this means.
Each entry records four things: the problem, the fix, the impact (especially any data loss), and any remaining uncertainty. This log becomes essential when you revisit the analysis weeks later or when someone else needs to understand your decisions.






NotePublic Interest Context: Openness



The Known Issues Log is a transparency tool. In public interest data work, publishing your data problems alongside your conclusions is what openness looks like in practice. It is the difference between presenting findings as if they emerged cleanly from perfect data and acknowledging that every real-world dataset has quirks, and that you made judgment calls about how to handle them.
Openness does not mean you must apologize for your data. Every analysis has limitations. It means you make your limitations visible so that readers can evaluate them — and so that you can be held accountable for the decisions you made. A Known Issues Log entry that says “23 rows with party='OTH' were excluded because the category is ambiguous” is far more honest and defensible than leaving the exclusion unmentioned.
See Chapter 7 for the broader framework connecting openness to public interest data practice.




3.5.3 The Data Dictionary
A data dictionary describes what each column in your dataset contains. It is the single most useful piece of documentation you can write, and it takes only a few minutes. Here is a Markdown table format that works well in notebooks:
| Column | Type | Description | Valid Values | Cleaning Applied |
|--------|------|-------------|--------------|------------------|
| county | str  | County name | 64 CO counties | .str.strip().str.upper() |
| year   | int  | Calendar year | 2014–2024 | pd.to_numeric() |
| sales  | float | Sales in USD | >= 0 | Removed $ and commas |
The data dictionary should be updated whenever you add derived columns or recode values. It is a living document, not a one-time artifact.

title: 3  Documentation and Markdown
text:
3.6 Comments in Code
Markdown cells handle the narrative layer of documentation. Code comments handle the technical layer — explaining why a particular line of code does what it does, not what it does (which should be clear from the code itself).
# Good comment: explains WHY
# Replace 0 AB with NaN to avoid division-by-zero in batting average
batting['BA'] = batting['H'] / batting['AB'].replace(0, float('nan'))

# Bad comment: restates WHAT the code does
# Divide H by AB
batting['BA'] = batting['H'] / batting['AB']
A useful heuristic: if the code does exactly what it says — df.head(), print(x) — it does not need a comment. If the code involves a non-obvious decision — why you chose errors='coerce' instead of errors='raise', why you filtered to rows with more than 100 at-bats, why you used a left join instead of an inner join — that is worth a comment.

title: 3  Documentation and Markdown
text:
3.7 Common Issues in This Chapter






WarningYour Markdown renders as raw text instead of formatted output



What it means: The cell in Jupyter is set to “Code” instead of “Markdown,” so the asterisks and pound signs appear literally.
How to fix it: Select the cell and press M (when in command mode, not edit mode) or use the dropdown at the top of the notebook to switch it to Markdown. Re-run the cell with Shift+Enter.








WarningYour Known Issues Log gets out of sync with the code



What it means: You cleaned something, then forgot to log it — weeks later you can’t tell why your row count changed.
How to fix it: Build the habit of writing the log entry before running the cleaning cell, not after. The log entry is a plan; the code is the execution.








WarningYour notebook is all code with no narrative



What it means: You have a working analysis but no one reading it — including you in three months — can tell what question you were answering or what you concluded.
How to fix it: Add a top-level Markdown cell with the research question. Before each section of code, add a one-sentence Markdown cell that states the goal. After each result, add a one-sentence interpretation. You will be surprised how much this helps.

title: 3  Documentation and Markdown
text:
3.8 Key Takeaways

Documentation is a habit, not a final step. The Known Issues Log and Data Dictionary are living documents you maintain while working.
Markdown is tiny — headings, emphasis, lists, links, code blocks, tables — and covers almost everything you need for data documentation.
Code comments should explain why you made a decision, not what a line of code does.
A notebook with interleaved narrative and code tells a story. A notebook with only code is a puzzle your future self has to solve.

title: 3  Documentation and Markdown
text:
3.9 Further Reading






Tip📚 Going deeper on documentation and Markdown






Markdown Guide — markdownguide.org is the cleanest reference for the syntax. The “Cheat Sheet” page is worth bookmarking.
GitHub Flavored Markdown Spec — github.github.com/gfm/ documents GitHub’s specific Markdown dialect, including task lists and tables.
Jupyter Notebook Markdown cells — jupyter-notebook.readthedocs.io describes how Markdown is rendered inside notebooks.

title: 3  Documentation and Markdown
text:
3.10 Exercises

Open one of the course datasets and identify a data quality issue (a column with mixed types, an unexpected value, missing data in a key field). Write a Known Issues Log entry for it using the four-part structure: Problem, Fix, Impact, Remaining Issue.

Choose a dataset you have worked with recently. Write a data dictionary as a Markdown table that describes every column, its type, valid values, and any cleaning that was applied. If you have not cleaned the data yet, note what cleaning you think will be needed.









NoteExercise 3: Document a complete workflow





Create a Jupyter notebook that loads the Colorado cannabis sales data and produces the following documentation artifacts — all visible in the notebook as rendered Markdown cells:

The goal is a notebook that a classmate could open, read the Markdown cells, and understand what you found and why you made the choices you made.

title: 4  Python Environments
text:
4.1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:

title: 4  Python Environments
text:
Explain what a Python environment is and why it matters for reproducibility.
Use conda to list, install, and update packages in your active environment.
Distinguish between conda and pip and decide which to use when.
Diagnose ImportError / ModuleNotFoundError by comparing your notebook’s kernel to the environment where the package is installed.
Verify a working SQLite installation on macOS, Windows, and Linux.

title: 4  Python Environments
text:
4.2 What an Environment Is
When you installed Python — probably through Anaconda — you installed the Python interpreter along with hundreds of packages that come bundled with it. Pandas, NumPy, Matplotlib, Jupyter, and many others were all placed into a specific directory on your computer. This collection of a Python interpreter and its associated packages is called an environment.
Understanding environments matters because the tools in this book depend on specific packages being installed and accessible. When something does not work, the problem is often that the wrong environment is active, that a package is missing, or that two packages have conflicting version requirements. This chapter gives you enough knowledge to diagnose and fix these problems.

title: 4  Python Environments
text:
4.3 Anaconda and Conda
Anaconda is a distribution of Python designed for data science. It includes the conda package manager, which handles installing, updating, and removing packages. You have been using conda implicitly every time you open Anaconda Navigator or Anaconda Prompt.
To check which version of conda you have, open a terminal and run:
conda --version
To see which packages are installed in your current environment:
conda list
To install a new package:
conda install pandas
To update a package:
conda update pandas

title: 4  Python Environments
text:
4.4 The Base Environment
When you install Anaconda, it creates a default environment called base. This is the environment that activates when you open Anaconda Prompt. For the purposes of this book, the base environment contains everything you need.
You can verify which environment is active by looking at your terminal prompt — it typically shows the environment name in parentheses:
(base) $
If you see a different name, or no parentheses at all, you may be in a different environment or conda may not be activated.

title: 4  Python Environments
text:
4.5 Pip: The Other Package Manager
Python has a second package manager called pip. While conda manages both Python packages and non-Python dependencies (like C libraries), pip installs only Python packages from the Python Package Index (PyPI). In an Anaconda environment, prefer conda install when possible, and fall back to pip install only for packages that conda does not carry.
pip install some-package






Warning



Mixing conda and pip in the same environment can occasionally cause conflicts. If you must use pip, do so after installing everything you can with conda.

title: 4  Python Environments
text:
4.6 Checking What Is Installed
When a notebook gives you an ImportError or ModuleNotFoundError, it means the package is not installed in your current environment. To check:
conda list | grep pandas    # Mac/Linux
conda list | findstr pandas  # Windows
If the package is missing, install it with conda install package-name.

title: 4  Python Environments
text:
4.7 Jupyter and Kernels
When you open a Jupyter notebook, it runs code using a kernel — a specific Python environment. If you have multiple environments, each one can have its own kernel. A common source of confusion is having a package installed in one environment but running your notebook with a kernel from a different environment.
This is a surprisingly common problem. You install pandas with conda install pandas in your base environment, but Jupyter is running with a different kernel — and the install is invisible to it. The result is a ModuleNotFoundError even though you are confident pandas is installed somewhere.
To check which Python your notebook is using, run this in a code cell:
import sys
print(sys.executable)   # full path to the Python interpreter this kernel uses
print(sys.version)      # Python version number
The output path should point to the Python interpreter inside your intended environment. If it points to a path you do not recognize, or a different environment than you expected, switch the kernel using the Kernel menu (Jupyter Notebook) or the kernel picker in the top-right corner (VS Code or JupyterLab).

4.7.1 Verifying the active environment matches the kernel
A more complete check compares the environment name in your terminal prompt with the kernel path reported by sys.executable:
import sys
import pandas   # will fail with ImportError if pandas is not in this kernel's env

print('Python location:', sys.executable)
print('pandas version:', pandas.__version__)
If this cell runs without error, the kernel has access to pandas and is in a working state. If it fails, install the package in the environment the kernel is pointing to, not just any environment.

title: 4  Python Environments
text:
4.8 SQLite Installation
SQLite is used in the SQL chapters of this book. On macOS and Linux, SQLite is typically preinstalled. To check:
sqlite3 --version
On Windows, SQLite may not be installed by default. You can install it through conda:
conda install -c anaconda sqlite
Alternatively, download the precompiled binaries from sqlite.org/download.html, unzip the archive, and add the folder to your system PATH. After editing the PATH, open a new terminal window — the old window will not see the change.

title: 4  Python Environments
text:
4.9 Common Issues in This Chapter






Warningconda: command not found (or 'conda' is not recognized)



What it means: Your terminal cannot find the conda executable. On Windows, you are probably in Command Prompt or PowerShell instead of Anaconda Prompt. On macOS/Linux, conda is not on your PATH.
How to fix it: On Windows, open Anaconda Prompt from the Start menu. On macOS/Linux, run the one-time conda init command (see the conda docs) and restart your terminal.








WarningModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'pandas'



What it means: The package is not installed in the environment your notebook is using — which may not be the environment you installed it in.
How to fix it: Run import sys; print(sys.executable) in a notebook cell. Copy that path. Then in your terminal, confirm that path matches the environment where you installed pandas. If they differ, either install pandas in the notebook’s environment or switch the notebook’s kernel to the environment that has pandas.








WarningA package installed fine but still fails to import



What it means: The running Jupyter kernel was started before the install finished, so it is still pointing at the old environment state.
How to fix it: Restart the Jupyter kernel (Kernel → Restart) and re-run your imports. This is one of the most common “ghost” errors and is almost always solved by a kernel restart.








WarningConda is very slow to resolve an install



What it means: Conda is trying to solve a complex dependency graph. For large data-science environments, this can take minutes.
How to fix it: Wait. If it still hangs after several minutes, consider using mamba — a faster drop-in replacement for conda — or install a newer version of conda that uses the libmamba solver by default.

title: 4  Python Environments
text:
4.10 Key Takeaways

An environment is a specific Python interpreter plus a set of installed packages. Your notebook is always running in some environment; when things break, identifying which one is the first question.
Prefer conda install for scientific Python packages; fall back to pip install when conda does not carry a package.
ImportError / ModuleNotFoundError almost always means “wrong environment” or “kernel not restarted,” not “package broken.”
sys.executable inside a notebook is the definitive answer to “which Python am I actually running?”

title: 4  Python Environments
text:
4.11 Further Reading






Tip📚 Going deeper on Python environments






Conda user guide — docs.conda.io/projects/conda/en/stable/user-guide/ is the definitive reference. Start with “Getting started with conda” and “Managing environments.”
Pip user guide — pip.pypa.io/en/stable/user_guide/ documents pip for the cases where you need it.
Mamba — mamba.readthedocs.io is a faster, compatible alternative to conda for complex installs.
Real Python: Setting Up Python for Machine Learning on Windows — realpython.com/python-windows-machine-learning-setup/ is an especially useful walkthrough for Windows users.
Jupyter kernels — jupyter.readthedocs.io/en/latest/install/kernels.html explains how to register additional kernels for other environments.

title: 4  Python Environments
text:
4.12 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: Environment audit





Open a terminal and run conda list. Verify that pandas, numpy, matplotlib, and jupyter are all installed. Check the version of each. Then open a Jupyter notebook and run import pandas; print(pandas.__version__) to confirm the notebook is using the same version.









NoteExercise 2: Install SQLite





Follow the instructions above to verify that SQLite is installed on your system. Run sqlite3 --version in a terminal. If it is not found, install it using conda or by downloading the binaries. Create a test database by running sqlite3 test.db ".tables" and verify that the file test.db appears in your directory.









NoteExercise 3: Kernel mismatch diagnosis





Open a new Jupyter notebook and run the following in a code cell:
import sys
print('Python executable:', sys.executable)
print('Python version:', sys.version)

import pandas
print('pandas version:', pandas.__version__)

What path does sys.executable report? Does it contain the name of your conda environment (e.g., base)?
Open a terminal and run conda activate base (or your environment name), then conda list | grep pandas. Does the version match what the notebook reported?
If you can switch the notebook to a different kernel (from the Kernel menu or kernel picker), do so and re-run the cell. Did the path and version change?

Describe in two sentences what you learned about how Jupyter selects which Python to use.

title: 5  Reading Error Messages
text:
5.1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:

title: 5  Reading Error Messages
text:
Read a Python traceback from the bottom up and identify the error type, error message, and line of your code that caused it.
Recognize and respond to the most common Python exceptions: FileNotFoundError, KeyError, TypeError, ValueError, AttributeError, and ModuleNotFoundError.
Interpret pandas’ DtypeWarning and decide whether it indicates a real problem.
Apply a systematic five-step debugging process rather than changing things at random.

title: 5  Reading Error Messages
text:
5.2 Errors Are Information
Error messages are not punishments. They are diagnostic information — often the most useful information you will receive when something goes wrong. Learning to read them carefully and systematically is one of the highest-leverage skills you can develop as a programmer. This chapter teaches you how to extract actionable information from Python error messages rather than treating them as walls of intimidating red text.
If seeing red text on your screen makes your stomach drop, you are in good company. Every programmer feels that at some point. The difference between a beginner and an expert is not that experts stop getting errors — they get plenty — but that they have built the habit of reading what the error actually says before reacting to the feeling of having broken something.

title: 5  Reading Error Messages
text:
5.3 Anatomy of a Python Error
When Python encounters a problem, it produces a traceback — a report that shows the sequence of function calls that led to the error, ending with the specific error type and message. Here is a typical example:
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
FileNotFoundError                         Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[3], line 1
----> 1 df = pd.read_csv('my_data.csv')

File ~/anaconda3/lib/python3.11/site-packages/pandas/io/parsers/readers.py:948
    948     return _read(filepath_or_buffer, kwds)

FileNotFoundError: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: 'my_data.csv'
Read this from the bottom up. The last line tells you what went wrong: Python could not find a file called my_data.csv. The lines above tell you where the error occurred: in your notebook cell, line 1, in the call to pd.read_csv(). The middle lines show the internal pandas code that was running when the error happened — you can usually skip these.
The two most important pieces of information are always at the bottom: the error type (e.g., FileNotFoundError) and the error message (e.g., No such file or directory: 'my_data.csv').

title: 5  Reading Error Messages
text:
5.4 Common Error Types

5.4.1 FileNotFoundError
FileNotFoundError: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: 'my_data.csv'
What it means: Python looked for the file at the path you specified and could not find it. This does not mean the file does not exist — it means it does not exist at that location relative to where your code is running.
How to fix it: Check that the file name is spelled correctly, including capitalization. Verify your working directory with import os; print(os.getcwd()). Make sure the file is in the directory you expect. If the file is in a subdirectory, use a relative path like data/raw/my_data.csv.


5.4.2 KeyError
KeyError: 'County'
What it means: You tried to access a column (or dictionary key) that does not exist. This is almost always a spelling or capitalization problem.
How to fix it: Print df.columns.tolist() to see the exact column names, including any hidden whitespace. Common culprits include trailing spaces ('County ' vs 'County'), inconsistent capitalization ('county' vs 'County'), and unexpected characters from the source file.


5.4.3 TypeError
TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for +: 'int' and 'str'
What it means: You tried to perform an operation on a data type that does not support it — for example, adding a number and a string together. In pandas, this often happens when a column you expected to be numeric is actually stored as text because it contains non-numeric values like $1,234 or N/A.
How to fix it: Check the column’s dtype with df['column'].dtype. If it shows object when you expected a number, inspect the values with df['column'].unique() to find the non-numeric entries, then clean them before converting.


5.4.4 ValueError
ValueError: cannot convert float NaN to integer
What it means: A conversion or operation received a value it cannot handle. This commonly occurs when you try to cast a column containing NaN values to integer type, since regular Python integers cannot represent missing values.
How to fix it: Either fill or drop the NaN values before converting, or use pandas’ nullable integer type (Int64 with a capital I) which can hold missing values.


5.4.5 AttributeError
AttributeError: 'DataFrame' object has no attribute 'str'
What it means: You tried to use a method that does not exist on the object you called it on. The .str accessor, for example, only works on a Series, not on an entire DataFrame.
How to fix it: Check that you are calling the method on the right object. If you meant to apply .str.strip() to a column, make sure you are selecting the column first: df['column'].str.strip(), not df.str.strip().


5.4.6 ModuleNotFoundError
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'pandas'
What it means: Python cannot find the package you are trying to import. Either the package is not installed, or your notebook is running in a different environment from the one where you installed it.
How to fix it: Install the package with conda install pandas or pip install pandas. If you already installed it, verify that your notebook is using the correct Python environment (see Chapter 4).

title: 5  Reading Error Messages
text:
5.5 The DtypeWarning
This is not an error — it is a warning — but it is so common and so important that it deserves special attention:
DtypeWarning: Columns (3) have mixed types. Specify dtype option on import or set low_memory=False.
What it means: Pandas found both numeric and non-numeric values in the same column. It defaulted to storing everything as strings (object type), which means your arithmetic operations will not work as expected.
How to fix it: Investigate the column to find the non-numeric values, then decide whether to clean them or specify the column’s type explicitly at load time with the dtype parameter of read_csv.

title: 5  Reading Error Messages
text:
5.6 A Systematic Debugging Process
When you encounter an error, resist the urge to randomly change things and re-run. Instead, follow this process:

Read the last line of the traceback. What is the error type? What does the message say?
Read the first arrow line. Which line of your code caused the error?
Reproduce minimally. Can you trigger the same error with a simpler version of the operation? If you were running a complex chain of methods, try each step individually.
Inspect the inputs. Print the shape, dtype, and a sample of the data going into the operation that failed. Is it what you expected?
Search with the error message. Copy the error type and message into a search engine. Someone has almost certainly encountered the same problem.







TipA mindset shift



Beginners often feel that getting an error means they did something wrong. Experienced programmers get errors constantly — the difference is that they read the error message, understand what it is telling them, and use it to fix the problem. Errors are information, not failures.

title: 5  Reading Error Messages
text:
5.7 Key Takeaways

Read from the bottom up. The last line of a traceback tells you the error type and message. The first “arrow” line (---->) tells you which line of your code triggered it.
FileNotFoundError, KeyError, and TypeError account for most of the errors you will encounter in this book. Build reflex fixes for each.
Reproduce minimally. If a chained expression fails, break it into single steps so you can see exactly where the break is.
Search the exact error message. Someone else has almost always hit the same problem before; Stack Overflow is faster than guessing.
Errors are not failures — they are feedback. The same message that feels like a wall today will feel like a map once you have read a few dozen of them.

title: 5  Reading Error Messages
text:
5.8 Further Reading






Tip📚 Going deeper on Python errors and debugging






Python Built-in Exceptions — docs.python.org/3/library/exceptions.html lists every exception type in the standard library with a short description of what triggers it.
Python Errors and Exceptions tutorial — docs.python.org/3/tutorial/errors.html is the official tutorial on handling errors, including try/except.
The pdb debugger — docs.python.org/3/library/pdb.html documents Python’s built-in interactive debugger, which lets you step through code one line at a time.
Real Python: Python Exceptions: An Introduction — realpython.com/python-exceptions/ is an accessible walkthrough with examples.
pandas: Common pitfalls — pandas.pydata.org/docs/user_guide/gotchas.html describes pandas-specific behavior that commonly surprises newcomers.

title: 5  Reading Error Messages
text:
5.9 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: Diagnose from a traceback





Given the following traceback, identify: (a) the error type, (b) the specific problem described in the message, (c) which line of user code caused the error, and (d) what you would do to fix it.
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
KeyError                                  Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[7], line 1
----> 1 df['Retial_Sales'].sum()

KeyError: 'Retial_Sales'






NoteSolution






KeyError. (b) The column 'Retial_Sales' does not exist — note the typo: “Retial” instead of “Retail”. (c) Line 1 of cell 7. (d) Correct the column name to 'Retail_Sales' (or whatever it is — check with df.columns.tolist() to see the exact names).













NoteExercise 2: Trigger and fix common errors





Write code that deliberately triggers each error type below, then fix it. For each, write a one-sentence explanation of what caused the error and what the fix was.

Load the cannabis sales data without any read_csv parameters. Try to sum the Retail Sales column. You will get a TypeError. What caused it, and how do you fix it?
Try to access a column named 'retail_sales' (lowercase) on the DataFrame you just loaded. You will get a KeyError. Why? What does df.columns.tolist() show you?
After loading the cannabis data with header=2 and assigning column names, try to convert the Month column to integer using .astype(int) on the raw (uncleaned) version of the DataFrame. If any values prevent conversion, you will get a ValueError. What does the error message tell you?







NoteSolution






Without na_values=['NR'], the Retail Sales column is loaded as object dtype because it contains both currency strings and the text NR. Summing strings raises a TypeError. Fix: add na_values=['NR'] and strip currency symbols before converting to numeric.
The raw column name includes a space: 'Retail Sales', not 'retail_sales'. df.columns.tolist() shows the exact names. Fix: use the exact name, or rename columns with df.columns = ['Month', 'Year', 'County', 'Medical', 'Retail'].
If the Month column has any non-integer values (e.g., leftover header text from the title block), .astype(int) raises a ValueError. Fix: use pd.to_numeric(df['Month'], errors='coerce') to handle unconvertible values.













NoteExercise 3: Apply the systematic debugging process





A classmate shows you the following code and says “it runs but gives the wrong answer”:
df = pd.read_csv('colorado_cannabis_sales_by_county.csv', header=2, skipfooter=8)
total = df['Retail Sales'].sum()
print(f"Total retail sales: {total}")
The output is Total retail sales: 0. Using the five-step debugging process from this chapter, identify what went wrong. What would you inspect, and what is the likely cause of the zero result?






NoteSolution





Step 4 (inspect the inputs) is the key step here. Run print(df['Retail Sales'].dtype) — it almost certainly shows object because the column contains currency strings like $1,234,567. Summing strings returns 0 (or raises a TypeError depending on the pandas version). Step 3 (reproduce minimally): df['Retail Sales'].head() confirms the values are formatted as strings. The fix is to strip $ and , characters and convert to numeric with pd.to_numeric(). This error produces a wrong answer with no error message — a “silent error” that is more dangerous than an exception.

text:
6.1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:

title: 6  Research Questions and Study Designs
section: 6.2 Why This Chapter Comes Before the Code
text:
6.2 Why This Chapter Comes Before the Code
The technical chapters ahead teach you to load, clean, extend, and reshape data. Those are essential mechanical skills. But mechanics alone do not produce insight — you also need to ask the right questions and understand what your data can and cannot tell you. This chapter introduces the conceptual vocabulary for formulating research questions with tabular data, recognizing the structure of the comparisons your data supports, and identifying the limitations that prevent you from drawing conclusions the data does not warrant.
Reading this chapter first — before writing a line of pandas or SQL — gives you a frame for the technical work ahead. The datasets you will use, and the analyses you will eventually run, are shaped by the questions you are trying to answer.
None of this requires new code. It requires new thinking.
crumbs: 6  Research Questions and Study Designs

title: 6  Research Questions and Study Designs
section: 6.3 What Makes a Good Research Question?
text:
6.3 What Makes a Good Research Question?
A good research question is specific enough to be answered with data and interesting enough to be worth answering. Compare these two questions:

“Is baseball different now?” — too vague to answer. Different how? Compared to when?
“Has the league-wide home run rate per at-bat changed between the 1990s and the 2010s?” — specific, measurable, and answerable with the Lahman batting data.

A useful template is: “Is there a difference in [outcome] between [groups] / over [time] / across [conditions]?” The components of this template — outcome, groups, time, conditions — map directly to columns in your data. If you cannot point to specific columns that represent each component, the question is not yet answerable with the data you have.
crumbs: 6  Research Questions and Study Designs

title: 6  Research Questions and Study Designs
text:
6.4 The Unit of Analysis
Before you can answer any question, you need to know what a single row in your dataset represents. This is the unit of analysis, and getting it wrong is one of the most common sources of error in data work.
In the Lahman batting data, each row is a player-season-stint: one player’s statistics for one team in one season. If you compute the average batting average across all rows, you are computing the average across player-seasons — which weights a player with 10 at-bats the same as a player with 600. That is almost certainly not what you intend.
In the Colorado population data, each row is a county-year-age: one county’s population for one single year of age in one year. If you sum totalpopulation without grouping by county and year first, you will add up every age group and get a number far larger than any county’s actual population.
The discipline from Parts II and III — always checking .shape, always understanding what a row represents — is more than a cleaning habit. It is the foundation of valid analysis.








Dataset
Unit of analysis
Common mistake




Lahman Batting
Player-team-season
Averaging rates without weighting by at-bats


DOLA Population
County-year-age
Summing population without grouping by county-year


Cannabis Sales
County-month-year
Treating monthly data as annual without aggregation


CDC Deaths
County-year-age group-cause
Double-counting deaths across cause categories
crumbs: 6  Research Questions and Study Designs

title: 6  Research Questions and Study Designs
text:
6.5 Between-Subjects vs. Within-Subjects Comparisons
Most analytical questions involve comparisons, and the structure of the comparison determines which methods are appropriate.
Between-subjects (or between-groups) comparisons look at different entities measured under different conditions. “Do urban counties have higher crime rates than rural counties?” compares different counties — each county is measured once, and the comparison is across groups. In tabular data, this means filtering to different subsets of rows and comparing an outcome column.
Within-subjects (or repeated-measures) comparisons look at the same entity measured at multiple time points or under multiple conditions. “Has Boulder County’s dependency ratio changed from 2000 to 2020?” tracks a single county across time. In tabular data, this means filtering to one entity and examining how an outcome column changes across another column (usually time).
The distinction matters because between-subjects comparisons are confounded by all the ways the groups differ beyond the variable of interest. Urban and rural counties differ in population density, but they also differ in demographics, economic base, healthcare access, and dozens of other factors. A within-subjects comparison controls for many of these differences (Boulder County’s geography does not change between 2000 and 2020), but introduces the possibility that other things changed simultaneously (state policy, national trends).

6.5.1 Recognizing the structure in your data
Ask yourself: “Am I comparing different entities, or the same entity at different times?”

import pandas as pd

# Between-subjects: compare counties in a single year
# Each county appears once → between-subjects
# pop_2020 = pop[pop['year'] == 2020].groupby('county')['totalpopulation'].sum()

# Within-subjects: track one county across years
# Same county appears multiple times → within-subjects
# boulder_trend = pop[pop['county'] == 'Boulder'].groupby('year')['totalpopulation'].sum()
crumbs: 6  Research Questions and Study Designs

title: 6  Research Questions and Study Designs
text:
6.6 Cross-Sectional vs. Longitudinal Analysis
A closely related distinction is whether your analysis examines one point in time or multiple points.
Cross-sectional analysis looks at a snapshot: all counties in 2020, all players in the 2022 season. It is good for describing the current state of things and identifying patterns across entities, but it cannot tell you about change over time.
Longitudinal analysis tracks the same entities across time: Boulder County from 1990 to 2024, league-wide batting averages from 1900 to 2022. It can reveal trends, turning points, and the effects of interventions (a new policy, a rule change), but it requires careful handling of time-related confounds.
Most of the datasets in this book support both types of analysis. The cannabis sales data can be analyzed cross-sectionally (which counties had the highest sales in 2023?) or longitudinally (how have Denver’s sales changed since legalization?). The choice depends on the question, not the data.
crumbs: 6  Research Questions and Study Designs

title: 6  Research Questions and Study Designs
section: 6.7 Observational Data and the Limits of Causation
text:
6.7 Observational Data and the Limits of Causation
Every dataset in this book is observational — the data was collected by recording what happened, not by experimentally manipulating conditions and measuring the effect. This is the norm in data analysis outside of controlled experiments, and it imposes a fundamental limitation: observational data can show associations, but it cannot prove causation.
Consider the merged Lahman dataset from Chapter 11, where you found that higher-paid players tend to have better batting statistics. Can you conclude that paying players more causes them to hit better? No. Teams pay more for players who have already demonstrated high performance. The causal arrow likely runs from performance to salary, not the other way around. And both might be influenced by a third factor — age, position, market size — that you have not accounted for.
This does not mean observational analysis is useless. It means you must be disciplined about the language you use. Say “counties with higher population density tend to have higher crime rates” rather than “high population density causes higher crime rates.” The first is a description of a pattern in the data. The second is a causal claim that the data cannot support.

6.7.1 Common confounds in the course datasets







Apparent relationship
Possible confound




Higher salary → better batting stats
Teams pay for past performance; age and experience drive both


More cannabis sales → more crime
Both correlate with county population; urban counties have more of both


Higher dependency ratio → lower income
Rural counties have both older populations and less economic diversity


More deaths → larger county
Death counts scale with population; rates are the meaningful comparison



The last example deserves emphasis. Comparing raw counts across groups of different sizes is one of the most common analytical mistakes. Denver will always have more deaths, more crimes, and more cannabis sales than San Juan County — because Denver has 700,000 people and San Juan has 700. The meaningful comparison is the rate: deaths per 100,000 population, crimes per capita, sales per resident. Computing rates requires the population data, which is why the multi-table join in Chapter 15 is more than a technical exercise — it is an analytical necessity.
crumbs: 6  Research Questions and Study Designs

title: 6  Research Questions and Study Designs
text:
6.8 Formulating Your Question
Before writing any analytical code, answer these four questions in a Markdown cell:

What is the outcome I am measuring? (Which column? What does a higher or lower value mean?)
What am I comparing? (Groups? Time periods? Conditions?)
What is the unit of analysis? (What does each row represent? Do I need to aggregate first?)
What can I conclude vs. what can I only speculate about? (Association or causation? What confounds exist?)

This takes two minutes and prevents hours of misguided analysis. The remaining chapters in Part IV assume you have done this work before running any code.
crumbs: 6  Research Questions and Study Designs

title: 6  Research Questions and Study Designs
text:
6.9 Common Issues in This Chapter






WarningYour research question is too broad to answer



What it means: You wrote something like “Is housing broken?” — there is no specific column you can point to as the outcome, and no clear group you are comparing.
How to fix it: Rewrite using the “outcome across groups / time / conditions” template. Force yourself to name the specific column that represents the outcome and the specific filter that defines the groups. If you cannot, the question is not yet answerable with the data you have.








WarningYour summary statistic does not mean what you think it means



What it means: You computed a mean, median, or sum without accounting for the unit of analysis. Averaging batting averages across player-seasons, or summing population across age groups, gives a number that sounds meaningful but is not.
How to fix it: Before any aggregation, write down what a single row represents. Decide whether you need to aggregate to a different unit first. If you are averaging rates, decide whether to weight by the underlying sample size.








WarningYou compared raw counts across unequal populations



What it means: Denver has more of everything than San Juan County. Conclusions based on raw counts are really conclusions about population size.
How to fix it: Convert to per-capita rates before comparing. “Deaths per 100,000 population” is almost always the right denominator for cross-county comparisons.








WarningYou slipped into causal language



What it means: Your write-up says “X causes Y” when the data only shows that X and Y are correlated.
How to fix it: Reread your conclusions and replace causal verbs (causes, leads to, drives) with correlational language (is associated with, tends to co-occur with). Name at least one plausible confound explicitly so the reader knows you considered it.
crumbs: 6  Research Questions and Study Designs

title: 6  Research Questions and Study Designs
text:
6.10 Key Takeaways

A good research question names a specific outcome, a specific comparison, and a specific unit of analysis.
The unit of analysis — what a single row represents — determines which aggregations are meaningful. Checking .shape is more than hygiene; it is analytical discipline.
Between-subjects vs. within-subjects and cross-sectional vs. longitudinal are two independent distinctions. Know which you are doing before you start.
Observational data supports associations, not causal claims. Use language that matches what your data can actually show.
Per-capita rates, not raw counts, make cross-group comparisons meaningful.

text:
6.11 Further Reading






Tip📚 Going deeper on research questions and study design






“A Field Guide to Lies and Statistics” by Daniel Levitin — an accessible introduction to common statistical fallacies in public reporting.
“Causal Inference: The Mixtape” by Scott Cunningham — mixtape.scunning.com is a free textbook on causal inference, written for people with modest math backgrounds.
Data Skeptic podcast — dataskeptic.com regularly discusses study design and the limits of data-driven conclusions.
