title: 6  Research Questions and Study Designs
text:
6.12 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: Classify the comparison





For each question below, identify whether it is a between-subjects or within-subjects comparison, and whether it is cross-sectional or longitudinal.

Do National League teams hit more home runs than American League teams in 2022?
Has Colorado’s statewide dependency ratio increased from 2000 to 2020?
Is there a correlation between county population and cannabis sales in 2023?
Did batting averages decline after the introduction of the designated hitter rule?







NoteSolution






Between-subjects (different teams), cross-sectional (one season). b. Within-subjects (same state), longitudinal (multiple years). c. Between-subjects (different counties), cross-sectional (one year). d. Within-subjects in a sense (same league), longitudinal (before/after a rule change) — though it is comparing different players across eras, making it partly between-subjects too.













NoteExercise 2: Identify the confound





A colleague shows you a scatter plot demonstrating that Colorado counties with more cannabis dispensaries also have more traffic accidents. They conclude that cannabis causes traffic accidents. Write a paragraph identifying at least two confounds and explaining why this conclusion is not supported by the data.






NoteSolution





Both dispensary count and traffic accidents correlate with county population — larger counties have more of everything. They also correlate with urbanization, road density, and commuter traffic. Without controlling for population (converting to per-capita rates) and other covariates, the correlation between dispensaries and accidents may be entirely explained by the fact that both are proxies for county size. Observational data like this can show association but cannot establish causation.












NoteExercise 3: Formulate a research question





Choose one of the course datasets and write a research question using the template from this chapter. Then answer the four framing questions (outcome, comparison, unit of analysis, limitations). Be specific enough that another student could run the analysis from your description alone.

title: 7  Public Interest Data Science
text:
7.1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:

title: 7  Public Interest Data Science
text:
Identify three dynamics — enclosure, erosion, and exemption — that shape what public data looks like and what it omits.
Recognize the telltale symptoms of each dynamic in real datasets (title blocks, format mismatches, suppression codes, absent records).
Apply the three pillars of public interest practice — openness, oversight, and ownership — using the Known Issues Log, join-quality reports, and Data Dictionary.
Frame your analytical conclusions in language that matches what the data can actually support.

title: 7  Public Interest Data Science
text:
7.2 Why This Chapter Matters
Data analysis is never neutral. Every dataset reflects choices about what to count, how to count it, who gets to count, and who is counted. This chapter introduces a framework for thinking about those choices — not to make you suspicious of data, but to make you a more careful reader of it.
The datasets in this book come from government agencies, public records, and open archives. They are about cannabis sales, voter registration, crime, mortality, and population. They are, in a meaningful sense, about people. Understanding how they were produced — and what was left out — is part of doing the analysis well.

title: 7  Public Interest Data Science
section: 7.3 Three Dynamics That Shape Public Data
text:
7.3 Three Dynamics That Shape Public Data
Public data is shaped by three recurring dynamics that appear, in different forms, in nearly every dataset you will work with in this book.

7.3.1 Enclosure: Data Designed for Humans, Not Machines
Enclosure happens when data that is technically public is formatted in ways that make it difficult to use programmatically. The data exists, but accessing it requires significant effort — and that effort is unevenly distributed.
The Colorado cannabis sales file is a clear example. It is published by a state agency and available to download, but it arrives as an Excel export with a two-row title block at the top, eight footnote rows at the bottom, and sales figures formatted as currency strings ($1,234,567). None of this matters if you are reading the spreadsheet in Excel. All of it matters if you are trying to load it into pandas.
The CDC WONDER database is a more extreme example. Mortality data is available, but it must be requested through a web form that limits what you can query at once. The results are delivered as HTML tables — not CSV files, not an API — which means extracting the data requires additional parsing steps that are not documented or supported.
These are not accidents. They are the result of publishing workflows optimized for human readers in browsers and spreadsheets. The practical effect is that the people who can most easily use this data are those with the technical skills to work around its formatting — a form of enclosure through interface design.






NoteWhat to look for



When you load a new dataset, the problems you encounter in the first few cells — extra header rows, currency strings, aggregated totals, merged cells — are often symptoms of enclosure. The dataset was designed for a different audience than you.




7.3.2 Erosion: Infrastructure Decay Made Concrete
Erosion happens when data infrastructure degrades over time, typically because no single agency is responsible for maintaining consistency across systems.
The county name mismatch in the course datasets is a concrete example. Three Colorado government agencies each publish data about Colorado counties:

The CDC WONDER database formats county names as "Adams County, CO".
The Colorado Bureau of Investigation uses "Adams County".
The Colorado State Demography Office uses bare names: "Adams".

No one decided that these formats should differ. They are the accumulated result of three agencies building their own systems, at different times, with different conventions and no coordination mandate. The mismatch is invisible until you try to join the tables — at which point it silently drops every row, because "Adams County, CO" does not equal "Adams County" does not equal "Adams".
This is infrastructure erosion made concrete: a technical inconvenience that embodies a real governance problem. The cost is borne by anyone who tries to analyze these datasets together — which is exactly the analysis you would want to do if you were studying, say, the relationship between crime rates, death rates, and population in Colorado counties.






NoteWhat to look for



When joining tables from different agencies, always audit your key columns before joining. SELECT DISTINCT county FROM deaths ORDER BY county; run against all three tables will immediately reveal format mismatches that would otherwise produce a silent empty result.




7.3.3 Exemption: What Data Does Not Exist
Exemption is the hardest dynamic to see, because it involves absence rather than presence. It happens when data that would be useful for public accountability is not collected, not retained, or not released.
The CDC suppression rule is a visible example of exemption by policy. When death counts in a county-year-cause cell are below 10, the CDC reports the value as "Suppressed" rather than a number. The stated reason is privacy protection — individuals in a small cell might be identifiable. This is a legitimate concern, but the effect is that the counties with the smallest populations — often rural counties, often with higher rates of specific causes of death — have the most missing data. The places where data would be most informative are the places where data is least available.
Less visible forms of exemption include data that is never collected at all: housing displacement records at the parcel level, detention facility population data, stop-and-frisk incident logs in jurisdictions that do not publish them. These absences are not accidents either. They reflect choices about what is worth measuring and who is worth measuring it for.






NoteWhat to look for



When a column has missing values, ask why. "Suppressed" in the CDC data is not a data quality problem — it is a policy decision. Understanding why data is missing is different from knowing that it is missing, and it matters for how you handle it analytically and how you communicate about it.

title: 7  Public Interest Data Science
section: 7.4 Three Pillars of Public Interest Data Practice
text:
7.4 Three Pillars of Public Interest Data Practice
Understanding these dynamics does not make data analysis impossible. It makes it more honest. Three practices, which appear throughout this book, help translate awareness into better work.

7.4.1 Openness — The Known Issues Log
Openness means making your data problems visible rather than hiding them. The Known Issues Log introduced in Chapter 3 is a transparency tool: a running record of every quality problem you found, what you decided to do about it, and why.
Publishing a Known Issues Log alongside your analysis lets readers evaluate your decisions rather than taking your conclusions on faith. It also protects you: documenting that you knew about a data problem and addressed it deliberately is very different from having it discovered later as an unacknowledged flaw.
Openness is not about being embarrassed by data problems. Every real-world dataset has them. It is about treating data quality as a fact to communicate rather than a weakness to conceal.


7.4.2 Oversight — The Join-Quality Report
Oversight means auditing your data pipelines to catch silent failures. The join-quality report introduced in Chapter 15 is an audit trail: a structured check of what matched, what was dropped, and whether the result is plausible.
A join that silently drops 40% of rows because of a county name format mismatch is not a software bug — it is a logical error that produces a confident-looking wrong answer. Oversight practices — checking row counts before and after joins, validating totals, auditing unmatched records — are what prevent silent errors from becoming invisible conclusions.
In a public interest context, the stakes are higher. An analysis of county-level health outcomes that silently drops rural counties is not only technically wrong — it is systematically biased against the populations that are hardest to count.


7.4.3 Ownership — The Data Dictionary
Ownership means tracking provenance: where data came from, who collected it, when it was accessed, and what decisions were made about it. The Data Dictionary introduced in Chapter 3 is a provenance record.
Provenance matters for reproducibility — someone else should be able to reconstruct your analysis from the same sources. It also matters for accountability. Knowing that a dataset comes from the Colorado Department of Revenue’s Marijuana Enforcement Division, downloaded on a specific date from a specific URL, is different from knowing only that you “got the cannabis data from the state.”
In an era of frequently updated government datasets, provenance records also let you know whether your analysis is based on data that has since been revised or retracted.

title: 7  Public Interest Data Science
text:
7.6 Common Issues in This Chapter






WarningYou treat missing values as random



What it means: Code that drops NaNs or fills them with a mean assumes the missingness is random. When the missingness is systematic — the CDC suppression rule, for example — that assumption biases your analysis.
How to fix it: Before any missing-value imputation, ask why the value is missing. Document the answer in your Known Issues Log. Treat policy-driven suppression differently from data-entry omissions.








WarningA join silently drops rows because of format mismatches



What it means: You joined two agency tables on county, but one uses "Adams County, CO" and the other uses "Adams". The result is confident-looking, systematically wrong.
How to fix it: Run a join-quality report (Chapter 15): compare row counts before and after the join, and list any unmatched keys. Standardize keys in a cleaning step before joining.








WarningYour analysis does not account for absent data



What it means: The counties or demographic groups with the most missing rows are often the ones your analysis would most benefit from including. Silence about absence is not the same as representativeness.
How to fix it: When you drop or exclude rows, document what was excluded and speculate about what the exclusion means for your conclusions. A limitation section in your writeup is not weakness — it is credibility.

title: 7  Public Interest Data Science
text:
7.7 Key Takeaways

Public data is shaped by three recurring dynamics — enclosure, erosion, and exemption — that are invisible until you know to look for them.
Three practices — openness (Known Issues Log), oversight (join-quality reports), and ownership (Data Dictionary) — translate awareness into better analysis.
The technical habits in this book (inspect before acting, validate after every transform, document your decisions) are not separate from ethics. They are what ethical data practice looks like in code.
Good data work makes absent data and uncertain decisions visible, not invisible.

text:
7.8 Further Reading

title: 8  Loading Data with pandas
text:
8.1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:

title: 8  Loading Data with pandas
text:
Load CSV and Excel files into a pandas DataFrame using pd.read_csv() and pd.read_excel().
Control how pandas interprets a file using header, skipfooter, na_values, sep, dtype, and encoding.
Run the post-load checklist — shape, headers, types, missingness, distributions, duplicates, plausibility, and a quick visual scan — on every new dataset.

Navigate pandas’ documentation using help(), the ? operator, and the online API reference.

title: 8  Loading Data with pandas
section: 8.2 Why Loading Is Harder Than It Looks
text:
8.2 Why Loading Is Harder Than It Looks
Every analysis begins with loading data — getting information from a file on disk into a pandas DataFrame where you can inspect and manipulate it. This chapter covers the mechanics of loading CSV and Excel files using pd.read_csv() and pd.read_excel(), the parameters that control how pandas interprets the file, and the post-load inspection routine that catches problems before they propagate into your analysis.
Loading data is where most silent errors originate. A column that looks numeric but was loaded as text, a header row mistakenly treated as data, a delimiter that splits values in the wrong places — these problems are invisible unless you actively look for them. The discipline of inspecting data immediately after loading is one of the most valuable habits you will develop in this course.
This chapter builds on the project-organization and terminal skills from Chapter 2 and Chapter 1. If a pd.read_csv call fails with FileNotFoundError, review those chapters — the fix is almost always a path or working-directory issue.

title: 8  Loading Data with pandas
text:
8.3 Setup
You will use three libraries in this chapter: pandas for the DataFrame, numpy for numerical arrays, and matplotlib for the quick visualization at the end.

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt







WarningIf you get an ImportError on any of these





If you see ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'pandas' (or numpy, or matplotlib), the package is not installed in the Python environment your notebook is using. Install it with:
conda install pandas numpy matplotlib
Or, if you are not using conda:
pip install pandas numpy matplotlib
After installing, restart the Jupyter kernel (Kernel → Restart) and rerun the import cell. If the error persists, the notebook’s kernel is probably pointing at a different environment than the one you installed into — see Chapter 4 for how to check sys.executable and align the two. Full reference: Chapter 5.

title: 8  Loading Data with pandas
text:
8.4 How to Read pandas Documentation
Before you run your first load, take a minute to learn how to read the documentation. You will come back to the docs constantly — not because you are a beginner, but because experienced data scientists do the same. Nobody memorizes every parameter of pd.read_csv (there are more than 30).
There are three ways to access documentation from inside a notebook:

The ? operator (IPython / Jupyter only): type pd.read_csv? in a cell and run it. A help panel appears with the function’s signature and docstring.
The help() function (works anywhere): help(pd.read_csv) prints the same information as text.
The online docs: pandas.pydata.org/docs/reference/api/pandas.read_csv.html shows the same content formatted for the web, plus examples and cross-references.

A function signature looks like this:
pd.read_csv(filepath_or_buffer, *, sep=NoDefault.no_default, header='infer',
            names=None, index_col=None, usecols=None, dtype=None,
            engine=None, ..., encoding=None, skipfooter=0, ...)
The first argument (filepath_or_buffer) is required — you must pass it. Everything after the * is a keyword argument — it has a default value and you can leave it out unless you need to change it. The documentation tells you what each parameter does, what types it accepts, and what the default behavior is.






TipDocs are a reference, not a tutorial



Pandas documentation is terse by design. It describes behavior, not motivation. When you are learning a new function, skim the signature and the first paragraph, then jump to the examples at the bottom — that is usually the fastest way to understand what the function does. Come back to the parameter list when you hit a specific problem.

title: 8  Loading Data with pandas
text:
8.5 A First Load
The Colorado Department of Revenue publishes monthly cannabis sales by county. The data is distributed as a CSV file originally exported from Excel, which means it has some quirks we need to handle. Let’s start with a naive load and see what happens.






TipBefore you run pd.read_csv: where is the file?



pd.read_csv('colorado_cannabis_sales_by_county.csv') looks for the file in your notebook’s current working directory. If you get a FileNotFoundError, the file is not there — the file name is mistyped, the file lives in a subdirectory (data/raw/colorado_cannabis_sales_by_county.csv), or your notebook is running in a different folder than you expected. Run import os; print(os.getcwd()) to confirm where you are. See Chapter 5 for a deeper walkthrough of FileNotFoundError.



df = pd.read_csv('colorado_cannabis_sales_by_county.csv')

Before doing anything else, look at the data.

df.shape


df.head()


df.tail(10)

Something is wrong. The real column headers appear to be on row 2, not row 0. The first two rows contain a title block from the original Excel file. And the bottom of the file likely contains footnotes or totals that are not data rows.
This is a common pattern with government data — the files are formatted for human reading, not for machine ingestion. Your job is to tell pandas how to read the file correctly.






NotePublic Interest Context: Enclosure by Design



The cannabis sales file is technically public — it is published by a Colorado state agency and freely downloadable. But its formatting serves a different audience than you. The two-row title block, the eight-row footnote section, and the currency-formatted numbers are there because the file was designed for humans reading it in Excel, not for analysts loading it into pandas.
This pattern — public data formatted for display, not for analysis — is common in government publishing. It is a form of enclosure: the data exists, but accessing it programmatically requires significant additional work. That work is unevenly distributed; people with fewer technical resources find it harder to use the same public data.
The NR values in the sales columns are a different form of enclosure. The Colorado Department of Revenue suppresses sales figures for counties with fewer than three active licensees, citing privacy protection for those businesses. The policy is legitimate, but the result is that the counties with the least cannabis activity — often smaller, rural counties — are also the ones with the least data. See Chapter 7 for the broader framework.

title: 8  Loading Data with pandas
text:
8.6 The Post-Load Checklist
Before you touch any data, run through this checklist. It takes less than a minute and catches the majority of loading problems.

Shape: df.shape — does the row and column count match expectations?
Headers: df.columns.tolist() — are column names unique, non-empty, and meaningful?
Types: df.dtypes — are numbers numeric, dates datetime, and text object?
Missingness: df.isna().sum() — how many missing values per column?
Distributions: df['col'].value_counts() — do categorical values look right?
Duplicates: df.duplicated(subset=[key_cols]).sum() — is there a candidate key?
Plausibility: df.describe() — any impossible ranges, negative counts, future dates?
Quick look: df.head(), df.tail(), df.sample(5) — do rows look right?

You will apply this checklist in every loading chapter in this book, in both Python and SQL. Memorize it.

title: 8  Loading Data with pandas
section: 8.7 Fixing the Load with read_csv Parameters
text:
8.7 Fixing the Load with read_csv Parameters
The pd.read_csv() function has dozens of parameters that control how the file is interpreted. For most real-world files, you will need to specify at least a few of them. Here are the most important ones.

8.7.1 header: where the column names are
If the header row is not the first row of the file, specify its zero-indexed position:

df = pd.read_csv(
    'colorado_cannabis_sales_by_county.csv',
    header=2
)

df.head()



8.7.2 skipfooter: removing trailing rows
Government data files often have footnotes, totals, or metadata appended to the bottom. The skipfooter parameter removes a specified number of rows from the end. Note that skipfooter requires setting engine='python' because the default C parser does not support it.

df = pd.read_csv(
    'colorado_cannabis_sales_by_county.csv',
    header=2,
    skipfooter=8,
    engine='python'
)

df.tail()



8.7.3 na_values: declaring custom missing value codes
Many datasets use codes like NR (Not Reported), -999, ., or empty strings to represent missing values. The na_values parameter tells pandas to treat these as NaN:

df = pd.read_csv(
    'colorado_cannabis_sales_by_county.csv',
    header=2,
    skipfooter=8,
    engine='python',
    na_values=['NR']
)

df.tail()



8.7.4 sep: specifying the delimiter
CSV stands for “comma-separated values,” but many files use other delimiters — tabs (\t), pipes (|), or semicolons (;). If your file uses something other than a comma, specify it with sep:

# Example: pipe-delimited file
# df = pd.read_csv('voter_data.txt', sep='|')



8.7.5 dtype: controlling column types
By default, pandas guesses each column’s type. You can override this with a dictionary mapping column names to types. This is especially useful for columns that look numeric but should remain strings — like zip codes or ID numbers that have leading zeros:

# Example: preserve leading zeros in zip codes
# df = pd.read_csv('data.csv', dtype={'zip_code': str, 'voter_id': str})

title: 8  Loading Data with pandas
text:
8.8 Building Up Iteratively
Notice the pattern above: you load the file, check the result, add a parameter to fix a problem, and reload. This iterative approach — get something to work, then move on to the next issue — is how experienced analysts work with messy files.
In a finished notebook, you would consolidate everything into a single cell. But when you are first working with a new dataset, spread the steps across multiple cells so you can see the effect of each parameter.

title: 8  Loading Data with pandas
section: 8.9 What the Parameters Fixed — and What Remains
text:
8.9 What the Parameters Fixed — and What Remains
The read_csv parameters handle structural problems at the boundary between file and DataFrame: they put the header in the right place, skip rows that are not data, and mark sentinel values as missing. Run the checklist again to see what they accomplished:






NoteFrom loading to cleaning



The column names, currency formatting, and statewide aggregate rows still need attention. Those are post-load cleaning operations — renaming columns, stripping currency symbols, filtering rows — covered in Chapter 9, which begins by completing this dataset before moving to more complex cleaning scenarios.

title: 8  Loading Data with pandas
text:
8.10 Running the Post-Load Checklist

print('Shape:', df.shape)
print('\nDtypes:')
print(df.dtypes)   # object dtype for numeric columns means they were loaded as strings
print('\nMissing values:')
print(df.isna().sum())  # count NaN values per column; NR entries should now show as NaN


# value_counts() for categorical columns — check that values look sensible
# (12 unique months, expected years, no garbled county names)
for col in ['Month', 'Year', 'County']:
    print(df[col].value_counts(), '\n\n')


# describe() for numeric-looking columns — note: these are still strings at this point
# so describe() shows string statistics (count, unique, top, freq) not min/max
for col in ['Medical', 'Retail']:
    print(df[col].describe(), '\n\n')

title: 8  Loading Data with pandas
section: 8.11 A Quick Preview: What You Can Do with Clean Data
text:
8.11 A Quick Preview: What You Can Do with Clean Data
With the data loaded and cleaned, you can immediately begin exploring. Here is a pivot table and a simple visualization — both of which we will cover in depth in later chapters:

# pivot_table() aggregates and reshapes in one step:
# index='County' → one row per county
# columns='Year' → one column per year
# values='Retail' → the cell values are retail sales
# aggfunc='sum' → sum all months within each county-year cell
pivot_df = pd.pivot_table(
    data=df,
    index='County',
    columns='Year',
    values='Retail',
    aggfunc='sum'
)

pivot_df.head()


# .loc['Las Animas'] selects the row for Las Animas County
# .plot() with a datetime-like index creates a line chart by default
pivot_df.loc['Las Animas'].plot(title='Las Animas County Retail Cannabis Sales by Year')

title: 8  Loading Data with pandas
text:
8.13 Common Issues in This Chapter






WarningFileNotFoundError: [Errno 2] No such file or directory



What it means: Pandas looked for the file at the path you gave and did not find it.
How to fix it: Run import os; print(os.getcwd()) to see where your notebook is looking. Confirm the file name spelling and extension. Use a relative path like '../data/raw/filename.csv' if the file lives in a sibling directory. See Chapter 5.








WarningDtypeWarning: Columns (N) have mixed types



What it means: Pandas found both numeric and non-numeric values in the same column and fell back to object (string) type. Your later arithmetic will silently fail.
How to fix it: Inspect the column with df['col'].value_counts(). If the mixing comes from a stray header row or footer, set skiprows or skipfooter. If the column has sentinel codes like NR or -999, pass them via na_values. If you want to force the type, use dtype={'col': str} (or float, int, etc.).








WarningUnicodeDecodeError: 'utf-8' codec can't decode byte



What it means: The file uses a non-UTF-8 encoding (common in files exported from older Windows software or government systems).
How to fix it: Try encoding='latin-1' or encoding='cp1252' in your pd.read_csv call. If you are on the command line, file my_data.csv (macOS/Linux) reports the encoding. Re-save the file as UTF-8 if you will use it repeatedly.








WarningNumbers import as strings because of $ or ,



What it means: A column like $1,234,567 is stored as text. df['Retail'] + 100 will concatenate a string rather than add a number.
How to fix it: Strip the currency/comma characters, then convert: df['Retail'] = pd.to_numeric(df['Retail'].str.replace(r'[$,]', '', regex=True), errors='coerce'). See Chapter 9 for the full pattern.

title: 8  Loading Data with pandas
text:
8.14 Key Takeaways

pd.read_csv and pd.read_excel have dozens of parameters; you rarely need all of them, but knowing header, skipfooter, na_values, sep, dtype, and encoding will cover most real-world files.
Run the post-load checklist on every new dataset: shape, headers, types, missingness, distributions, duplicates, plausibility, head/tail/sample.
Load → inspect → adjust → reload. This iterative pattern is how experienced analysts work with messy files.
Loading errors are the easiest class of errors to fix because the diagnostic message is usually precise. When in doubt, read the error, check the docs, and consult Chapter 5.
Documentation is a reference, not a tutorial. Get comfortable with help(), ?, and the online API page — they will save you hours over the course.

text:
8.16 Exercises

Load the Boulder County voter data file (boco_voter_data.txt), which uses a pipe (|) delimiter. Run the post-load checklist. How many rows and columns does it have? What are the column dtypes? Are there any columns that should be loaded as strings but were guessed as numeric?






NoteSolution






df = pd.read_csv('boco_voter_data.txt', sep='|')
print('Shape:', df.shape)
print('\nDtypes:')

df.head()

Load the Colorado State Demography Office population file (sya-county.csv). You may see a DtypeWarning about mixed types. Investigate which column triggered the warning by checking .dtypes and inspecting the column’s unique values. What non-numeric value is present, and what should you do about it?

print(sya_df.dtypes)
print(sya_df.head())

The DtypeWarning typically indicates that a column contains both numbers and text (such as a header row mixed into the data, or a metadata row). Investigate with .value_counts() and address by specifying dtype on import or by filtering out the problematic rows.

Choose one of the course datasets and write a single cell that loads it cleanly — specifying all necessary read_csv parameters, renaming columns to clean names, converting types as needed, and removing any non-data rows. End the cell by printing the shape and a sample of rows.

title: 9  Cleaning Data with pandas
text:
9.1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:

title: 9  Cleaning Data with pandas
text:
Convert string columns to numeric values using pd.to_numeric and interpret the errors parameter (coerce, ignore, raise).
Normalize text columns using the pandas .str accessor (.str.strip(), .str.upper(), .str.replace()).
Standardize categorical columns using .replace() and .map(), recognizing when each is appropriate.
Detect and handle duplicate rows with df.duplicated() and df.drop_duplicates().
Validate every transformation with before/after value_counts() and null-count comparisons.
Write reusable cleaning functions that return both the transformed data and a summary of what changed.

title: 9  Cleaning Data with pandas
text:
9.2 Why Cleaning Is the Work
Loading data gets it into your environment. Cleaning data makes it usable. This chapter covers the systematic process of identifying and fixing data quality problems — type mismatches, inconsistent categories, duplicate records, hidden sentinel values, and the ever-present challenge of missing data. The techniques here map directly to operations you may have performed in spreadsheets, but pandas gives you the ability to apply them reproducibly, document them precisely, and scale them to datasets too large for any spreadsheet.
The central discipline of this chapter is validation: after every transformation, you check that the data changed in the way you intended and that nothing was silently lost or corrupted.
If this chapter starts to feel like a lot of moving parts, that is a fair reaction. Data cleaning is not one technique — it is a collection of small patterns that you apply as needed. Expect to reread sections; expect to copy snippets; expect your first attempt at any new dataset to take longer than you thought. This is a normal part of becoming fluent.

title: 9  Cleaning Data with pandas
text:
9.3 Setup
This chapter uses pandas, numpy, and matplotlib.

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

pd.options.display.max_columns = 100
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt







WarningIf you get an ImportError on any of these





If any of numpy, pandas, or matplotlib is missing, install it with:
conda install numpy pandas matplotlib
Or with pip: pip install numpy pandas matplotlib. After installing, restart the kernel before re-running the import cell. See Chapter 4 for how to check which Python environment your notebook is using.

title: 9  Cleaning Data with pandas
text:
9.4 Completing the Cannabis Load
Before introducing the voter registration data, we complete the cannabis dataset cleaning started in Chapter 8. That chapter used read_csv parameters to fix the header, skip footers, and mark NR as missing. Four post-load operations remain: fixing column names, stripping whitespace, converting currency strings to numbers, and removing aggregate rows. This dataset is a simple cleaning scenario — each fix can be applied in a single line.

9.4.1 Fixing column names
The read_csv call in Chapter 8 produced auto-generated integer column names. Replace them with descriptive names:

import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv(
    'colorado_cannabis_sales_by_county.csv',
    header=2,
    skipfooter=8,
    engine='python',
    na_values=['NR']
)

df.columns = ['Month', 'Year', 'County', 'Medical', 'Retail']
df.tail()



9.4.2 Stripping whitespace
County names contain leading or trailing whitespace that would cause join failures later:

df['County'] = df['County'].str.strip()



9.4.3 Converting currency strings to numbers
The sales columns contain values like $1,234,567 — dollar signs and commas that prevent numeric operations. The .str.replace() method removes them, and pd.to_numeric() converts the result:

for col in ['Medical', 'Retail']:
    df[col] = pd.to_numeric(
        df[col]
        .str.replace('$', '', regex=False)   # remove dollar signs; regex=False means treat '$' as
        .str.replace(',', '', regex=False)   # a literal character, not a regex special character
        # pd.to_numeric() converts the cleaned string to float or int
        # NaN values (from na_values=['NR'] at load time) are preserved as NaN
    )

df.tail()







NoteThe leading-dot style



Notice the chain of .str.replace() calls written on separate lines. This “leading dot” style makes method chains easier to read and debug — you can comment out individual steps to isolate problems.




9.4.4 Removing aggregate rows
The dataset includes rows where County == 'Total' — monthly statewide totals that would corrupt any county-level analysis:

df = df[df['County'] != 'Total']



9.4.5 The complete load cell
In a finished notebook, all of these steps belong in a single reproducible cell:

df = pd.read_csv(
    'colorado_cannabis_sales_by_county.csv',
    header=2,
    skipfooter=8,
    engine='python',
    na_values=['NR']
)

df.columns = ['Month', 'Year', 'County', 'Medical', 'Retail']
df['County'] = df['County'].str.strip()

for col in ['Medical', 'Retail']:
    df[col] = pd.to_numeric(
        df[col]
        .str.replace('$', '', regex=False)
        .str.replace(',', '', regex=False)
    )

df = df[df['County'] != 'Total']
df.tail()

title: 9  Cleaning Data with pandas
text:
9.5 Loading the Voter Registration Data
We now turn to a more complex example. Boulder County voter registration data arrives as a pipe-delimited text file with a mix of data types, inconsistent categories, and the kinds of quality issues that appear in virtually every real-world dataset.

df = pd.read_csv('boco_voter_data.txt', sep='|')
df.head()

title: 9  Cleaning Data with pandas
text:
9.6 Numbers Stored as Text
One of the most common data quality problems is numeric values that pandas loaded as strings. This happens when a column contains any non-numeric values — even one — or when leading zeros need to be preserved (zip codes, ID numbers).
In a spreadsheet, you might notice this when numbers sort alphabetically (1, 10, 100, 2, 20) instead of numerically, or when formulas like SUM() return zero. In pandas, the symptom is a column with dtype: object that you expected to be numeric.

9.6.1 Forcing types at load time
The safest approach is to declare column types when you load the file, before pandas has a chance to guess:

dtype_d = {
    'RES_ZIP_CODE': str,
    'BALLOT_ZIP': str,
    'VOTER_ID': str,
    'PRECINCT': str,
}

df = pd.read_csv('boco_voter_data.txt', sep='|', dtype=dtype_d)



9.6.2 Converting after loading
If you need to convert a column from string to numeric after loading, use pd.to_numeric() with the errors parameter to control how conversion failures are handled:

df['YOB'] = pd.to_numeric(df['YOB'], errors='coerce')

The three options for errors each serve a different purpose. Using 'coerce' converts unparseable values to NaN, which is safe but loses information about what the original values were. Using 'ignore' leaves the column unchanged if any conversion fails, preserving data but keeping the column as strings. Using 'raise' throws an error on the first bad value, which is useful for debugging because it tells you exactly which value caused the problem.
Always check what you lost after coercing:

nulls_before = df['VOTER_ID'].isna().sum()
df['VOTER_ID_CLEAN'] = pd.to_numeric(df['VOTER_ID'], errors='coerce')
nulls_after = df['VOTER_ID_CLEAN'].isna().sum()
print(f"Conversion introduced {nulls_after - nulls_before} new nulls")

title: 9  Cleaning Data with pandas
text:
9.7 String Normalization
Text fields in real data are messy in predictable ways: inconsistent capitalization, extra whitespace, non-printing characters, and encoding artifacts. Pandas provides the .str accessor for vectorized string operations — the same operations you would apply with TRIM(), UPPER(), and CLEAN() in a spreadsheet, but applied to an entire column at once.

9.7.1 Trimming and case standardization

# Chaining .str methods in a single expression applies them left to right.
# Each method returns a new Series; the next method is called on that result.
# Writing each step on its own line makes it easy to comment out one step
# at a time to isolate which operation produces an unexpected result.
df['STREET_NAME'] = (
    df['STREET_NAME']
    .str.strip()                              # remove leading/trailing whitespace
    .str.upper()                              # standardize casing (avoids 'Main St' vs 'MAIN ST' mismatches)
    .str.replace(r'\s+', ' ', regex=True)     # collapse multiple internal spaces into one
)



9.7.2 The validation pattern
Always compare value_counts() before and after cleaning to verify the transformation did what you intended:

# Before cleaning
print(df['RES_CITY'].value_counts())

# After cleaning
df['RES_CITY'] = df['RES_CITY'].str.strip().str.upper()
print(df['RES_CITY'].value_counts())

# Check for unintended consequences
print('New nulls:', df['RES_CITY'].isna().sum())



9.7.3 Common string artifacts
Real-world data contains predictable string problems that survive import and need active cleaning. Extra whitespace includes more than leading and trailing spaces: it also includes non-breaking spaces (Unicode U+00A0), which are common in web-scraped data, and tabs or newlines embedded in fields. Inconsistent punctuation appears as variations like "Boulder, CO" vs. "Boulder CO" vs. "Boulder,CO", or "St. Louis" vs. "St Louis" vs. "Saint Louis". Encoding artifacts show up as garbled characters like "resumÃ©" (UTF-8 decoded as Latin-1) or "caf\xc3\xa9".

# Remove specific characters
df['VOTER_NAME'] = df['VOTER_NAME'].str.replace(',', '', regex=False)

# Replace non-breaking spaces with regular spaces
df['VOTER_NAME'] = df['VOTER_NAME'].str.replace('\xa0', ' ', regex=False)

title: 9  Cleaning Data with pandas
text:
9.8 Categorical Standardization
When a field has a controlled set of valid values — party affiliations, state abbreviations, product categories — the values in your data rarely match that set exactly. You will find abbreviations, misspellings, inconsistent capitalization, and historical codes that need to be mapped to a standard vocabulary.

9.8.1 Dictionary-based recoding with .replace()
The .replace() method takes a dictionary that maps old values to new values. Unmapped values are left unchanged:

df['PARTY'].value_counts()


party_map = {
    'DEM': 'DEM',
    'REP': 'REP',
    'UAF': 'UNA',
    'LBR': 'LBR',
    'GRN': 'GRN',
}

df['PARTY'] = df['PARTY'].str.strip().str.upper()
df['PARTY'] = df['PARTY'].replace(party_map)

# Check for unmapped values
print(df['PARTY'].value_counts())

Build the mapping dictionary by examining .value_counts() output, not by guessing. The data tells you which variants exist; your job is to decide which standard value each variant maps to.


9.8.2 .map() vs. .replace()
The .map() method is stricter: it replaces everything, and unmapped values become NaN. Use .map() when you have a controlled vocabulary and anything outside it is an error. Use .replace() when you want to update some values while leaving others untouched.

valid_parties = {
    'DEM': 'Democrat',
    'REP': 'Republican',
    'UNA': 'Unaffiliated',
    'LBR': 'Libertarian',
    'GRN': 'Green'
}

# .map() replaces every value using the dictionary. Values NOT in the dictionary
# become NaN — unlike .replace(), which leaves unmapped values unchanged.
# This strict behavior makes unmapped codes visible so you can investigate them.
df['PARTY_LABEL'] = df['PARTY'].map(valid_parties)

# Any NaN in PARTY_LABEL where PARTY is not NaN means the code had no mapping
# — either a new code or a variant not in our dictionary
missing_labels = df[df['PARTY_LABEL'].isna() & df['PARTY'].notna()]
print(missing_labels['PARTY'].value_counts())

title: 9  Cleaning Data with pandas
text:
9.9 Sentinel Values
Sentinel values are fake entries that masquerade as real data. They are placeholders inserted to represent missing information without leaving a cell blank. Common examples include -999, 9999, or -1 in numeric fields, and "N/A", ".", "-", or "unknown" in text fields. Dates like 01/01/1900 or 12/31/9999 are date sentinels.
You can declare sentinel values at load time:

# df = pd.read_csv('data.csv', na_values=['-999', '9999', '-1', 'N/A', '.', '-', 'unknown'])

Or replace them after loading:

sentinel_to_null = {-999: np.nan, 9999: np.nan, -1: np.nan}
# df = df.replace(sentinel_to_null)

title: 9  Cleaning Data with pandas
text:
9.10 Handling Duplicates
Duplicate records are rows that share the same values on one or more key columns. They arise from data entry errors, overlapping date ranges in source files, or deliberate denormalization. The critical question is always: are these duplicates errors, or do they represent legitimately repeated observations?

9.10.1 Identifying duplicates

# Fully identical rows
full_dupes = df.duplicated()
print(f"Fully identical rows: {full_dupes.sum()}")

# Duplicates on key columns
key_dupes = df.duplicated(subset=['VOTER_ID'], keep=False)
print(f"Rows with duplicate VOTER_ID: {key_dupes.sum()}")



9.10.2 Understanding the pattern
Before removing duplicates, examine what varies across the repeated rows:

key_counts = df['VOTER_ID'].value_counts()
example_id = key_counts[key_counts > 1].index[0]
example_rows = df[df['VOTER_ID'] == example_id]

for col in df.columns:
    if example_rows[col].nunique() > 1:
        print(f"{col} varies: {example_rows[col].unique()}")



9.10.3 Three strategies for deduplication
Keep first or last when a more recent record supersedes an earlier one:

# df_deduped = df.sort_values('SALE_DATE').drop_duplicates(subset=['ACCOUNTNO'], keep='last')

Aggregate when duplicates represent multiple measurements:

# df_agg = df.groupby('ACCOUNTNO', as_index=False).agg({'SALE_PRICE': 'last', 'SALE_DATE': 'max'})

Flag for review when you are not sure what is right:

df['is_duplicate'] = df.duplicated(subset=['VOTER_ID'], keep=False)
df_clean = df[~df['is_duplicate']]
df_review = df[df['is_duplicate']].sort_values('VOTER_ID')
print(f"Clean: {len(df_clean)}, Needs review: {len(df_review)}")

Always audit totals before and after deduplication to understand the impact.

title: 9  Cleaning Data with pandas
text:
9.11 Extracting Structured Data from Text
A common problem is multiple pieces of information crammed into one field — an address field containing street, city, state, and zip code, or a name field containing first and last names.

9.11.1 Splitting on a delimiter

# Split a name column into parts
df['VOTER_NAME'].str.split(' ', expand=True).head()

The expand=True parameter returns a DataFrame instead of a Series of lists, which makes it easy to assign the parts to new columns.


9.11.2 Regular expression extraction
For more complex patterns, use .str.extract() with a named capture group regex:

# Example: extract components from a structured address
# pattern = r'^(?P<street>.*?),?\s*(?P<city>[A-Za-z\s]+),?\s*(?P<state>[A-Z]{2})\s*(?P<zip>\d{5})$'
# extracted = df['full_address'].str.extract(pattern)

Always check the extraction success rate — how many rows matched the pattern? — and investigate the failures.

title: 9  Cleaning Data with pandas
text:
9.12 Building Reusable Cleaning Functions
When you find yourself repeating the same cleaning logic across multiple columns or datasets, wrap it in a function. Functions reduce copy-paste errors, make the cleaning logic self-documenting, and enable systematic testing.

def clean_currency_column(df, col_name, new_col_name=None):
    """
    Remove currency symbols and commas, convert to numeric.

    Parameters:
        df: DataFrame
        col_name: name of column to clean
        new_col_name: name for cleaned column (default: col_name + '_clean')

    Returns:
        DataFrame with new cleaned column added.
        Prints summary of changes.
    """
    if new_col_name is None:
        new_col_name = col_name + '_clean'

    nulls_before = df[col_name].isna().sum()

    cleaned = (
        df[col_name]
        .astype(str)
        .str.replace('$', '', regex=False)
        .str.replace(',', '', regex=False)
        .str.strip()
    )

    df[new_col_name] = pd.to_numeric(cleaned, errors='coerce')

    nulls_after = df[new_col_name].isna().sum()
    nulls_introduced = nulls_after - nulls_before

    print(f"Cleaned {col_name} → {new_col_name}")
    print(f"  Nulls before: {nulls_before}")
    print(f"  Nulls after: {nulls_after}")
    print(f"  Conversion failures: {nulls_introduced} ({nulls_introduced/len(df):.2%})")

    return df

title: 9  Cleaning Data with pandas
section: 9.13 Documentation: Known Issues Log and Data Dictionary
text:
9.13 Documentation: Known Issues Log and Data Dictionary
Every cleaning session should produce two documentation artifacts. The Known Issues Log records each problem you found, how you fixed it, and what impact the fix had — see Chapter 3 for the full format. The Data Dictionary describes every column in the cleaned dataset, including its type, valid values, and any cleaning that was applied.
These are not bureaucratic exercises. They are the only reliable way to remember your decisions when you return to the analysis later or when someone else needs to reproduce your work.






NotePublic Interest Context: Ownership



The Data Dictionary is a provenance record. It documents where the data came from, who collected it, what transformations were applied, and what the columns mean. In a public interest context, this kind of ownership documentation matters for two reasons.
First, it enables reproducibility. Someone else — a colleague, an instructor, a journalist — should be able to reconstruct your analysis from the same sources. If your Data Dictionary records that party was normalized using a specific mapping dictionary, and that mapping is in your notebook, the transformation is reproducible. If it is not documented, the cleaning is invisible and unverifiable.
Second, it enables accountability. The voter registration data comes from a specific county clerk’s office, exported on a specific date. The cleaning decisions you made — which party codes to consolidate, which rows to treat as duplicates, how to handle missing precincts — are your analytical choices, not facts in the data. Documenting them is how you distinguish your interpretation from the original record.
See Chapter 7 for the broader framework connecting ownership to public interest data practice.

title: 9  Cleaning Data with pandas
text:
9.14 Common Issues in This Chapter






WarningAttributeError: Can only use .str accessor with string values



What it means: You called .str.strip() (or similar) on a column whose dtype is not object. The column was probably loaded as numeric or mixed.
How to fix it: Check df['col'].dtype. If the column really is strings with one stray number, cast with .astype(str) first. If the column is genuinely numeric, you do not need the .str accessor at all.








Warningpd.to_numeric returns all NaN



What it means: Every value in the column failed to parse as a number — usually because a currency symbol or comma is still present.
How to fix it: Strip the currency characters first: df['col'].str.replace(r'[$,]', '', regex=True), then call pd.to_numeric(..., errors='coerce'). Inspect df['col'].unique()[:20] to confirm what values remain.








Warning.map() turned some values into NaN



What it means: .map() replaces every value using the dictionary — anything not in the dictionary becomes NaN. You almost certainly wanted .replace(), which only changes the mapped values and leaves the rest alone.
How to fix it: If you want to leave unmapped values untouched, switch to .replace(). If you want NaN as a way to detect unexpected values (a data-quality check), .map() is the right tool — document the unmapped values in your Known Issues Log.








Warningdrop_duplicates dropped more (or fewer) rows than expected



What it means: The default compares all columns. If you only care about a subset of identifying columns, you need to pass them explicitly.
How to fix it: Use df.drop_duplicates(subset=['VOTER_ID']). Always print df.shape before and after and inspect the diff. If the drop count surprises you, investigate a few of the dropped rows with df[df.duplicated(subset=['VOTER_ID'], keep=False)].

title: 9  Cleaning Data with pandas
text:
9.15 Key Takeaways

Every real dataset needs cleaning. The problems are predictable: mixed types, sentinel values, whitespace, inconsistent categories, duplicates.
Validate every transformation. Compare value_counts() and null counts before and after. A transformation that silently changes your row count is a bug waiting to surface downstream.
Use .replace() when you want to leave unmapped values alone; use .map() when anything outside your controlled vocabulary should become NaN.
Write reusable cleaning functions. If you are repeating the same five lines on three columns, that is a signal to abstract.
Your Known Issues Log and Data Dictionary are as important as the code itself — they record your analytical judgment so it can be reviewed later.

title: 9  Cleaning Data with pandas
text:
9.17 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: Clean the property data





Load the Boulder County residential assessment data (ar-rec-2025-residential.xlsx). Identify at least three data quality issues using the post-load checklist. Clean them, validating each transformation by comparing value_counts() or null counts before and after.






NoteSolution






arc_df = pd.read_excel('ar-rec-2025-residential.xlsx')
print('Shape:', arc_df.shape)
print('\nDtypes:')
print(arc_df.dtypes)

# Issue 1: ACCOUNTNO has trailing whitespace
arc_df['ACCOUNTNO'] = arc_df['ACCOUNTNO'].str.strip()

# Issue 2: Duplicates on ACCOUNTNO
print(f"\nDuplicate ACCOUNTNO values: {arc_df.duplicated(subset=['ACCOUNTNO']).sum()}")

# Issue 3: Check for mixed-type or sentinel values in numeric columns
print('\nSALE_PRICE sample:')
print(arc_df['SALE_PRICE'].describe())













NoteExercise 2: Write a categorical standardization function





Write a function called standardize_categorical() that takes a DataFrame, a column name, and a mapping dictionary, and returns the DataFrame with a new standardized column. The function should print a before-and-after comparison of value_counts() and report how many values were not covered by the mapping.






NoteSolution






def standardize_categorical(df, col_name, mapping_dict, new_col_name=None):
    if new_col_name is None:
        new_col_name = col_name + '_std'

    print(f"Before ({col_name}):")
    print(df[col_name].value_counts())

    df[new_col_name] = df[col_name].replace(mapping_dict)

    valid_values = set(mapping_dict.values())
    unmapped = df[~df[new_col_name].isin(valid_values)]

    print(f"\nAfter ({new_col_name}):")
    print(df[new_col_name].value_counts())
    print(f"\nUnmapped rows: {len(unmapped)} ({len(unmapped)/len(df):.1%})")

    return df













NoteExercise 3: Investigate duplicates and document your decision





Load the Boulder County voter registration data. Check for duplicate VOTER_ID values:

How many voter IDs appear more than once?
For one example of a duplicated voter ID, print all rows that share that ID. What columns differ between the duplicate rows? What columns are identical?
Write a Known Issues Log entry describing what you found, including: the number of affected rows, what you think caused the duplication (data entry error? administrative update?), and which deduplication strategy you chose.
Implement your chosen strategy (keep first, keep last, or flag for review). Verify the row count before and after.







NoteSolution






dtype_d = {'RES_ZIP_CODE': str, 'BALLOT_ZIP': str, 'VOTER_ID': str, 'PRECINCT': str}
df = pd.read_csv('boco_voter_data.txt', sep='|', dtype=dtype_d)

# 1. Count duplicated voter IDs
id_counts = df['VOTER_ID'].value_counts()
n_duped = (id_counts > 1).sum()
print(f"Voter IDs appearing more than once: {n_duped}")

# 2. Examine one example
if n_duped > 0:
    example_id = id_counts[id_counts > 1].index[0]
    example = df[df['VOTER_ID'] == example_id]
    for col in df.columns:
        if example[col].nunique() > 1:
            print(f"  {col} varies: {example[col].unique()}")

# 3. Strategy: keep last row (most recent entry likely has updated address)
# Known Issues Log: VOTER_ID duplication
# Problem: N voter IDs appear in multiple rows
# Fix: Retain the last row per VOTER_ID (assumed to be the most recent record)
# Impact: Reduced row count from X to Y
# Remaining issue: Cannot confirm which row reflects the true current state

n_before = len(df)
df_deduped = df.drop_duplicates(subset=['VOTER_ID'], keep='last')
n_after = len(df_deduped)
print(f"\nRows before: {n_before}, after deduplication: {n_after}, removed: {n_before - n_after}")

title: 10  Extending Data with pandas
text:
10.1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:

title: 10  Extending Data with pandas
text:
Create derived columns using arithmetic, conditional logic, and pd.cut binning.
Parse dates from integer columns using pd.to_datetime and apply time-series operations like resampling.
Compute grouped summaries using df.groupby() with .agg().
Choose between groupby().agg(), pivot_table, and pivot based on whether aggregation and reshaping are needed.
Distinguish .agg() (one row per group) from .transform() (same shape as input) and pick the right tool for a given question.

title: 10  Extending Data with pandas
text:
10.2 Why Extending Matters
Loading and cleaning produce a reliable dataset. Extending transforms that dataset into something that can answer questions. This chapter covers two core operations: creating derived columns from existing data, and computing grouped summaries that collapse many rows into meaningful aggregates. These are the programmatic equivalents of writing formulas and building pivot tables in a spreadsheet — but with the power to handle millions of rows, chain operations together, and reproduce every step exactly.
This chapter assumes you have completed Chapter 9. The batting and population data used here must be loaded and cleaned before extending.

title: 10  Extending Data with pandas
text:
10.3 Setup
This chapter uses pandas and numpy.

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

pd.set_option('display.max_columns', None)
pd.set_option('display.float_format', '{:.3f}'.format)







WarningIf you get an ImportError





Install missing packages with conda install pandas numpy (or pip install pandas numpy) and restart the Jupyter kernel. See Chapter 4 for how to verify your notebook’s kernel matches the environment where you installed.

title: 10  Extending Data with pandas
text:
10.4 Datasets
This chapter uses two datasets that illustrate different aspects of extending data.
The Lahman Baseball Database contains historical statistics for every Major League Baseball player and team. Each row in the Batting table represents one player’s performance for one team in one season. We will compute derived metrics like batting average and slugging percentage, then aggregate them across teams and decades.
The Colorado State Demography Office publishes single-year-of-age population estimates by county. Each row represents one county, one year, and one single-year age group. We will bin ages into demographic bands and compute the dependency ratio — a standard population metric.

batting = pd.read_csv('Batting.csv')

print('Shape:', batting.shape)
print('Year range:', batting['yearID'].min(), '–', batting['yearID'].max())
batting.head()


url = 'https://storage.googleapis.com/co-publicdata/sya-county.csv'
pop = pd.read_csv(url, skiprows=1, dtype={'countyfips': str})

print('Shape:', pop.shape)
print('Year range:', pop['year'].min(), '–', pop['year'].max())
print('Datatype values:')
print(pop['datatype'].value_counts())

title: 10  Extending Data with pandas
text:
10.5 Derived Columns
A derived column is a new column computed from existing columns using arithmetic, conditional logic, or binning. The key principle is that derived columns should be transparent — anyone reading your code should be able to see exactly how the new value was computed from the originals.

10.5.1 Arithmetic: Batting Average
Batting average is hits divided by at-bats. The formula is simple, but the implementation requires a decision about what to do when at-bats is zero:

# Replace 0 with NaN before dividing — a player with 0 at-bats has an
# undefined batting average, not a 0.000 average. Division by zero in
# pandas returns inf, which would corrupt any downstream calculations.
batting['BA'] = batting['H'] / batting['AB'].replace(0, float('nan'))

print('BA stats:')
print(batting['BA'].describe())
print('Rows with NaN BA:', batting['BA'].isna().sum())

Replacing zero with NaN before dividing avoids a ZeroDivisionError and produces NaN for players with no at-bats — which is the correct semantic meaning: their batting average is undefined, not zero.


10.5.2 Multi-step formulas: Slugging Percentage and OBP
More complex metrics require computing intermediate values. Slugging percentage measures batting productivity by weighting different types of hits:

batting['TB'] = (
    batting['H']
    + 2 * batting['2B']
    + 3 * batting['3B']
    + 4 * batting['HR']
)

batting['SLG'] = batting['TB'] / batting['AB'].replace(0, float('nan'))

On-base percentage measures how frequently a batter reaches base. It uses a formula with four variables in the numerator and denominator:
\[OBP = \frac{H + BB + HBP}{AB + BB + HBP + SF}\]

# HBP (hit by pitch) and SF (sacrifice fly) were not tracked in early seasons.
# Filling with 0 treats "not tracked" as "did not happen" — a documented
# assumption that should appear in the Known Issues Log.
batting['HBP'] = batting['HBP'].fillna(0)
batting['SF'] = batting['SF'].fillna(0)

# Build OBP numerator and denominator separately for readability
numerator = batting['H'] + batting['BB'] + batting['HBP']
denominator = batting['AB'] + batting['BB'] + batting['HBP'] + batting['SF']
batting['OBP'] = numerator / denominator

print(batting['OBP'].min(), batting['OBP'].max())

Notice the .fillna(0) calls. The HBP (hit by pitch) and SF (sacrifice fly) columns have missing values for early seasons when those statistics were not tracked. Filling with zero is a documented decision: we are treating “not tracked” as “did not happen.” This is debatable, and the Known Issues Log is where you would note this assumption.


10.5.3 Binning with pd.cut()
Binning transforms a continuous variable into categories. The Colorado population data has single-year ages (0 through 100); we want three demographic bands:

bins = [0, 18, 65, 101]   # bin edges: [0,18), [18,65), [65,101)
labels = ['Children (0-17)', 'Working Age (18-64)', 'Seniors (65+)']

# right=False makes intervals left-inclusive: age 18 belongs to "Working Age",
# not "Children". Without this, age 18 would fall in [0,18] and be a child.
# The upper bound 101 includes age 100 (the maximum in this dataset).
pop['age_band'] = pd.cut(pop['age'], bins=bins, labels=labels, right=False)

print('Age band counts:')
print(pop['age_band'].value_counts())

The right=False parameter makes bins left-inclusive: [0, 18) means ages 0 through 17. This puts 18-year-olds in the Working Age band, which is the standard demographic convention.
Always verify that no values fell outside the bins:

print('NaN age bands:', pop['age_band'].isna().sum())



10.5.4 Integer division for decades
A quick way to create decade bins from year values:

batting['decade'] = (batting['yearID'] // 10) * 10

print('Decades:')
print(batting['decade'].value_counts().sort_index())



10.5.5 Parsing Dates: Cannabis Sales
Government datasets frequently store dates as separate integer columns — Month and Year rather than a single date column. The cannabis sales data is a typical example. pd.to_datetime() can reconstruct a proper datetime from integer month and year values:

cannabis = pd.read_csv(
    'colorado_cannabis_sales_by_county.csv',
    header=2,
    skipfooter=8,
    engine='python',
    na_values=['NR']
)
cannabis.columns = ['Month', 'Year', 'County', 'Medical', 'Retail']
cannabis['County'] = cannabis['County'].str.strip()
for col in ['Medical', 'Retail']:
    cannabis[col] = pd.to_numeric(
        cannabis[col].str.replace('$', '', regex=False).str.replace(',', '', regex=False)
    )
cannabis = cannabis[cannabis['County'] != 'Total']

# pd.to_datetime() accepts a DataFrame with columns named 'year', 'month', 'day'.
# .assign(Day=1) adds a constant day-of-month (all dates become the 1st of the month).
# .rename() maps our capitalized column names to the lowercase names pd.to_datetime expects.
cannabis['date'] = pd.to_datetime(
    cannabis[['Year', 'Month']].assign(Day=1).rename(
        columns={'Year': 'year', 'Month': 'month'}
    )
)

print(cannabis[['Month', 'Year', 'date']].head())

pd.to_datetime() accepts a DataFrame with year, month, and day columns. The .assign(Day=1) adds a constant day-of-month, and .rename() converts the column names to lowercase as required.
Once parsed as a proper datetime, the column unlocks time-series operations — sorting chronologically, computing time differences, and resampling by period:

# .set_index('date') makes the date column the index so resample can use it
# .resample('QE') groups by quarter-end periods (Q1 ends March 31, etc.)
# .sum() aggregates all monthly values within each quarter
quarterly = (
    cannabis
    .set_index('date')
    ['Retail']
    .resample('QE')
    .sum()
)

print(quarterly.tail(8))







TipStoring dates as integers is not a mistake



Storing month and year as separate integer columns is a deliberate design in many government systems — it makes SQL filtering simple (WHERE year = 2022) without requiring date functions. When you encounter this pattern, pd.to_datetime() with assign(Day=1) is the standard reconstruction approach.

title: 10  Extending Data with pandas
text:
10.6 Grouped Summaries
The groupby() → agg() pattern is the programmatic equivalent of a spreadsheet pivot table. It splits the data into groups, applies an aggregation function to each group, and combines the results.

10.6.1 Basic groupby: Total HR per Team

latest_year = batting['yearID'].max()
yr_df = batting[batting['yearID'] == latest_year]

team_hr = (
    yr_df
    .groupby('teamID')
    .agg({'HR': 'sum'})
    .reset_index()
    .sort_values('HR', ascending=False)
)

team_hr.head()



10.6.2 Filtering before aggregating
When computing averages, filter to meaningful subsets first. A player with 2 at-bats and 1 hit has a .500 batting average, but that tells you nothing useful:

qualified = batting[batting['AB'] >= 100]

decade_ba = (
    qualified
    .groupby('decade')['BA']
    .agg('mean')
)

decade_ba.plot(title='Mean BA by Decade (players with ≥ 100 AB)')



10.6.3 The dependency ratio: a real-world grouped summary
The dependency ratio — the number of children and seniors per working-age adult — is a standard demographic metric that requires grouped aggregation to compute:

estimates = pop[pop['datatype'] == 'Estimate'].copy()
co_2020 = estimates[estimates['year'] == 2020]

band_summary = co_2020.groupby(['county', 'age_band']).agg(
    pop=('totalpopulation', 'sum')
).reset_index()

# Validate: grouped total should match original
print(f'Grouped total:  {band_summary["pop"].sum():,.0f}')
print(f'Original total: {co_2020["totalpopulation"].sum():,.0f}')

title: 10  Extending Data with pandas
text:
10.7 Three Tools, One Job
Pandas offers three tools for creating cross-tabular summaries, and understanding when to use each saves time and prevents errors.








Tool
Aggregates?
Use when




groupby().agg()
Yes
You need flexibility, named aggregations, or long format for further computation


pivot_table()
Yes
You want aggregation and reshaping in one step (closest to an Excel pivot)


pivot()
No
Data is already one-row-per-key; you only need to reshape




10.7.1 groupby().agg() — long format output

modern = batting[(batting['yearID'] >= 1990) & (batting['yearID'] <= 2022)].copy()

hr_long = (
    modern
    .groupby(['teamID', 'decade'])['HR']
    .agg('sum')
    .reset_index()
    .rename(columns={'HR': 'total_HR'})
)

print('Shape:', hr_long.shape, '← one row per (team, decade)')
hr_long.head()



10.7.2 pivot_table() — wide format in one step

hr_wide_pt = modern.pivot_table(
    index='teamID',
    columns='decade',
    values='HR',
    aggfunc='sum',
    fill_value=0
)

print('Shape:', hr_wide_pt.shape, '← one row per team, one column per decade')
hr_wide_pt.head()



10.7.3 pivot() — reshape only, no aggregation
pivot() requires every (index, columns) combination to appear exactly once in the data. It will raise a ValueError if there are duplicates, because it cannot aggregate:

# This WORKS because hr_long has one row per (team, decade)
hr_wide_pv = hr_long.pivot(
    index='teamID',
    columns='decade',
    values='total_HR'
)

print('Shape:', hr_wide_pv.shape)



10.7.4 Verifying all three agree

total_long = hr_long['total_HR'].sum()
total_pt = hr_wide_pt.values.sum()
total_pv = hr_wide_pv.fillna(0).values.sum()

print(f'groupby().agg() total HR: {total_long:,}')
print(f'pivot_table()   total HR: {total_pt:,}')
print(f'pivot()         total HR: {total_pv:,}')
print(f'All match: {total_long == total_pt == total_pv}')

title: 10  Extending Data with pandas
text:
10.8 agg() vs. transform()
Both operate on groups, but they produce fundamentally different outputs. The agg() method produces one row per group — the output is smaller than the input. The transform() method broadcasts the group-level result back to every row in the original DataFrame — the output has the same number of rows as the input.

# agg() — summary table
team_totals = (
    batting[batting['yearID'] == 2022]
    .groupby('teamID')['HR']
    .agg('sum')
    .reset_index()
)
print('agg() shape:', team_totals.shape, '(one row per team)')


# transform() — adds group stat to every row
batting['team_total_HR'] = (
    batting
    .groupby(['yearID', 'teamID'])['HR']
    .transform('sum')
)

batting['hr_share'] = batting['HR'] / batting['team_total_HR'].replace(0, float('nan'))

Use transform() when you want to compute something like “what percentage of team home runs did each player contribute?” — a question that requires both the individual value and the group total on the same row.

title: 10  Extending Data with pandas
text:
10.9 Common Issues in This Chapter






WarningZeroDivisionError when computing a rate



What it means: You divided by a column that contains zero. Batting average = hits / at-bats fails when at-bats is 0.
How to fix it: Replace the divisor’s zeros with NaN before dividing: df['H'] / df['AB'].replace(0, float('nan')). This produces NaN for undefined cases, which is the correct semantic.








Warningpivot() raises ValueError: Index contains duplicate entries



What it means: pivot() does not aggregate — it requires each (index, columns) combination to appear exactly once. If your source has multiple rows per combination, pivot() cannot decide which value to use.
How to fix it: Use pivot_table() instead, which takes an aggfunc parameter. Or aggregate first with groupby().agg() so each combination is unique, then call pivot().








Warninggroupby aggregation produces unexpected NaN



What it means: Groups with all-missing input produce NaN in the output, which is easy to misread as a zero.
How to fix it: Decide what missing should mean for your question. For counts, fill_value=0 in pivot_table or .fillna(0) after aggregation is usually appropriate. For means, leaving NaN is often correct — averaging nothing is not zero.








WarningGrouped totals do not match the original total



What it means: Either rows were filtered before grouping, or a groupby column has NaN values (which are excluded by default).
How to fix it: Compare df['col'].sum() before grouping and grouped['col'].sum() after. If they differ, inspect df['group_col'].isna().sum(). Use dropna=False in groupby if you want to keep missing-group rows in the output.

title: 10  Extending Data with pandas
text:
10.10 Key Takeaways

Derived columns should be transparent: reading the code should tell you exactly how a new value was computed. Avoid magic numbers and undocumented assumptions.
groupby().agg() → long format; pivot_table() → wide format in one step; pivot() → reshape only (no aggregation). Pick the tool that matches the question.
.agg() collapses a group to one row. .transform() broadcasts the group result back to every row. Use .transform() when the individual value and the group stat need to coexist.
Validate every aggregation by comparing totals before and after. Silent row drops — from NaN in a groupby key, or from missing data — are the most common source of wrong answers.
Binning continuous variables with pd.cut is a standard way to turn ages, incomes, or scores into analytically meaningful categories.

text:
