title: 10  Extending Data with pandas
text:
10.12 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: Compute OPS and identify top performers





OPS is On-base Plus Slugging: OPS = OBP + SLG. Add an OPS column to the batting DataFrame. Filter to players with at least 400 at-bats in a single season, and find the top 10 OPS seasons of all time.






NoteSolution






batting['OPS'] = batting['OBP'] + batting['SLG']

top_ops = (
    batting[batting['AB'] >= 400]
    .sort_values('OPS', ascending=False)
    [['playerID', 'yearID', 'teamID', 'AB', 'BA', 'SLG', 'OBP', 'OPS']]
    .head(10)
)
top_ops













NoteExercise 2: Compute the dependency ratio for all counties in 2020





Using the Colorado population data, filter to Estimate rows for 2020, bin ages into the three standard bands, and compute the dependency ratio for each county. Which county has the highest dependency ratio? The lowest?






NoteSolution






co_2020 = estimates[estimates['year'] == 2020].copy()

wide = co_2020.groupby(['county', 'age_band'], observed=True).agg(
    pop=('totalpopulation', 'sum')
).reset_index().pivot_table(
    index='county', columns='age_band', values='pop', aggfunc='sum'
).reset_index()
wide.columns.name = None

wide['dep_ratio'] = (
    (wide['Children (0-17)'] + wide['Seniors (65+)'])
    / wide['Working Age (18-64)']
)

print('Highest:', wide.sort_values('dep_ratio', ascending=False).head(3)[['county', 'dep_ratio']].to_string())
print('\nLowest:', wide.sort_values('dep_ratio').head(3)[['county', 'dep_ratio']].to_string())













NoteExercise 3: Compare pivot_table() and groupby() → pivot() paths





Using the dependency ratio data from Exercise 2, implement the same result using the two-step groupby().agg() → pivot() path. Verify that both approaches produce identical results using pd.testing.assert_frame_equal().






NoteSolution






# Path A: pivot_table (one step)
wide_pt = co_2020.pivot_table(
    index='county', columns='age_band', values='totalpopulation', aggfunc='sum'
)

# Path B: groupby → pivot (two steps)
long_agg = co_2020.groupby(['county', 'age_band'], observed=True).agg(
    pop=('totalpopulation', 'sum')
).reset_index()

wide_pv = long_agg.pivot(index='county', columns='age_band', values='pop')

# Verify
pd.testing.assert_frame_equal(
    wide_pt.sort_index(), wide_pv.sort_index(), check_names=False
)
print('Both paths produce identical DataFrames.')

title: 11  Reshaping Data with pandas
text:
11.1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:

title: 11  Reshaping Data with pandas
text:
Convert data between wide and long formats using pd.melt, pivot, and pivot_table.
Stack DataFrames vertically with pd.concat, validating row counts and column alignment.
Combine tables on shared keys using pd.merge with every join type (inner, left, right, outer).
Audit merge quality using the indicator=True parameter and understand why rows fail to match.
Recognize when aggregation must happen before a merge to avoid row inflation.

title: 11  Reshaping Data with pandas
text:
11.2 Why Reshaping Matters
Most analysis questions require data from multiple sources, and those sources rarely arrive in the shape you need. This chapter covers the operations that change the structure of your data: melt() to go from wide to long, pivot() and pivot_table() to go from long to wide, concat() to stack DataFrames vertically, and merge() to combine tables by matching on shared keys. Each operation carries risks — silent row inflation, dropped records, introduced nulls — that you must audit after every transformation.
Reshaping is where silent bugs love to hide. A merge that silently drops 40% of rows because of mismatched dtypes, a concat that creates duplicate columns because of trailing whitespace in a name, a pivot that aggregates values you did not expect to aggregate — all of these produce confident-looking output that is quietly wrong. Build the habit of auditing after every reshape.

title: 11  Reshaping Data with pandas
text:
11.3 Setup
This chapter uses pandas and numpy.

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

pd.set_option('display.max_columns', None)
pd.set_option('display.float_format', '{:.3f}'.format)







WarningIf you get an ImportError





Install missing packages with conda install pandas numpy or pip install pandas numpy, then restart the Jupyter kernel. See Chapter 4.

title: 11  Reshaping Data with pandas
text:
11.4 Datasets
This chapter continues with the Lahman Baseball Database and Colorado population data from Chapter 10, and adds two new Lahman tables — Salaries and People — to demonstrate multi-table merging.

batting = pd.read_csv('Batting.csv')

# Derived columns from previous chapter
batting['BA'] = batting['H'] / batting['AB'].replace(0, float('nan'))
batting['TB'] = batting['H'] + 2*batting['2B'] + 3*batting['3B'] + 4*batting['HR']
batting['SLG'] = batting['TB'] / batting['AB'].replace(0, float('nan'))
batting['HBP'] = batting['HBP'].fillna(0)
batting['SF'] = batting['SF'].fillna(0)
batting['OBP'] = (
    (batting['H'] + batting['BB'] + batting['HBP'])
    / (batting['AB'] + batting['BB'] + batting['HBP'] + batting['SF'])
    .replace(0, float('nan'))
)
batting['OPS'] = batting['OBP'] + batting['SLG']
batting['decade'] = (batting['yearID'] // 10) * 10

print('Batting shape:', batting.shape)


salaries = pd.read_csv('Salaries.csv')
print('Salaries shape:', salaries.shape)
print('Year range:', salaries['yearID'].min(), '–', salaries['yearID'].max())


people = pd.read_csv('People.csv')
print('People shape:', people.shape)
people[['playerID', 'nameFirst', 'nameLast', 'birthYear', 'birthState']].head()

title: 11  Reshaping Data with pandas
text:
11.5 Wide vs. Long Format
The same data can be represented in two fundamentally different shapes.
Wide format has one row per entity and multiple columns for repeated measurements. A table of HR by decade with teams as rows and decades as columns is wide format.
Long format (also called “tidy” format) has one row per observation. The same HR data in long format has one row per (team, decade) combination, with a single column for the HR value.
Neither format is inherently better. Wide format is easier for humans to read as a cross-tab. Long format is easier for computation — groupby(), merge(), and plotting functions generally expect long format. The skill is knowing how to convert between the two.

title: 11  Reshaping Data with pandas
text:
11.6 melt(): Wide to Long
The melt() function unpivots a wide DataFrame into long format. You specify which columns stay as identifiers (id_vars) and which columns get “melted” into a single key-value pair (value_vars).

# Build a wide pivot first
c0 = batting['yearID'] >= 1900
c1 = batting['yearID'] < 2020
batting_modern = batting[c0 & c1].copy()

hr_wide = batting_modern.pivot_table(
    index='decade',
    columns='teamID',
    values='HR',
    aggfunc='sum'
).reset_index()
hr_wide.columns.name = None

print('Wide shape:', hr_wide.shape)


team_cols = [c for c in hr_wide.columns if c != 'decade']  # all columns except the ID column

hr_long = hr_wide.melt(
    id_vars=['decade'],        # columns that stay as identifiers (not melted)
    value_vars=team_cols,      # columns to unpivot into rows (one row per team per decade)
    var_name='teamID',         # name for the new column that holds the old column headers
    value_name='total_HR'      # name for the new column that holds the old cell values
)

print('Long shape:', hr_long.shape)
# Expected: one row per (decade × team) combination
print('Expected rows:', hr_wide.shape[0], '×', len(team_cols), '=', hr_wide.shape[0] * len(team_cols))

After melting, some rows will contain NaN — a team that did not exist in a given decade. Always drop or flag these:

hr_clean = hr_long.dropna(subset=['total_HR'])
print('After dropping NaN:', hr_clean.shape)

The power of long format is that it enables further computation that would be awkward in wide format:

# League-wide mean HR per team by decade — easy in long, awkward in wide
league_avg = (
    hr_clean
    .groupby('decade')['total_HR']
    .agg(n_teams='count', mean_HR='mean', max_HR='max')
    .reset_index()
)

league_avg.set_index('decade')['mean_HR'].plot(title='Mean Team HR by Decade')

title: 11  Reshaping Data with pandas
text:
11.7 concat(): Stacking DataFrames
The concat() function appends rows from multiple DataFrames into one. This is the programmatic equivalent of copy-pasting rows across Excel sheets. Use it when you have multiple files with the same columns — annual exports, regional splits, or train/test splits.

dead_ball = batting[batting['yearID'] < 1920].copy()
live_ball = batting[(batting['yearID'] >= 1920) & (batting['yearID'] < 1970)].copy()
modern = batting[batting['yearID'] >= 1970].copy()

frames = [dead_ball, live_ball, modern]
# ignore_index=True resets the row index to 0, 1, 2, ... in the combined result.
# Without it, each DataFrame keeps its original index, creating a non-unique index
# that can cause subtle bugs later (e.g., .loc[0] returns multiple rows).
all_eras = pd.concat(frames, ignore_index=True)

print('Shape after concat:', all_eras.shape)
print('Row count matches original?', all_eras.shape[0] == batting.shape[0])







WarningThe validation pattern after concat()



Always check three things after concatenating: (1) row count equals the sum of input row counts, (2) no new NaN columns appeared (column name mismatches create them silently), and (3) no unexpected duplicates from overlapping date ranges.



11.7.1 Tracking provenance with keys
When files come from different sources, use the keys parameter to record which file each row came from:

era_labels = ['Dead Ball (<1920)', 'Live Ball (1920-1969)', 'Modern (1970+)']

all_eras_labeled = pd.concat(
    frames,
    keys=era_labels,
    ignore_index=False
).reset_index(level=0).rename(columns={'level_0': 'era'})

print(all_eras_labeled['era'].value_counts())

title: 11  Reshaping Data with pandas
text:
11.8 merge(): Combining Tables by Key
Merging is the most powerful and most dangerous reshaping operation. It combines two DataFrames by matching rows on one or more shared key columns — the programmatic equivalent of VLOOKUP in a spreadsheet, but with support for many-to-many relationships, multiple keys, and all join types.






WarningBefore you merge: check your column names and dtypes



The most common merge failure is a key that looks identical in both tables but actually differs. "Adams" vs "Adams ", "12345" (string) vs 12345 (int), or a typo in one column — any of these will silently drop rows.
Before merging, run print(df_left['key'].dtype, df_right['key'].dtype). If they differ, cast one side. Then run set(df_left['key']) - set(df_right['key']) to see unmatched values on the left, and the reverse for the right.



11.8.1 Step 1: Inspect keys before merging
Before any merge, verify that your keys have the same dtype in both tables and understand the cardinality — is the key unique in each table, or can it repeat?

print('Batting playerID dtype:', batting['playerID'].dtype)
print('Salaries playerID dtype:', salaries['playerID'].dtype)
print()
print('Batting: max rows per playerID+yearID:',
      batting.groupby(['playerID', 'yearID']).size().max(),
      '(>1 means multi-stint seasons)')
print('Salaries: max rows per playerID+yearID:',
      salaries.groupby(['playerID', 'yearID']).size().max())

The batting table has players with multiple rows per season — players traded mid-year get one row per team. This means a merge on playerID + yearID alone could produce multiple matches. The solution is to aggregate batting to one row per player-season first:

batting_season = (
    batting
    .groupby(['playerID', 'yearID'])
    .agg(
        AB=('AB', 'sum'), H=('H', 'sum'), TB=('TB', 'sum'),
        BB=('BB', 'sum'), HBP=('HBP', 'sum'), SF=('SF', 'sum'),
        HR=('HR', 'sum'), G=('G', 'sum')
    )
    .reset_index()
)

# Recompute rates on aggregated totals
batting_season['BA'] = batting_season['H'] / batting_season['AB'].replace(0, float('nan'))
batting_season['SLG'] = batting_season['TB'] / batting_season['AB'].replace(0, float('nan'))
batting_season['OPS'] = batting_season['BA'] + batting_season['SLG']  # simplified

print('Aggregated shape:', batting_season.shape, '(one row per player-season)')



11.8.2 Step 2: Merge with indicator=True
The indicator=True parameter adds a _merge column that shows whether each row matched in both tables, only in the left, or only in the right:

bat_sal = pd.merge(
    left=batting_season,
    right=salaries[['playerID', 'yearID', 'salary']],
    on=['playerID', 'yearID'],  # match rows where both playerID AND yearID agree
    how='left',      # LEFT JOIN: keep all rows from batting_season; add salary where available
    indicator=True   # add a '_merge' column showing each row's match status
)

print('Before merge:', batting_season.shape[0], 'rows')
# Row count should NOT change for a left merge — every batting row stays, salary is added alongside
print('After merge:', bat_sal.shape[0], 'rows')
print()
print('Merge audit:')
# 'both' = matched in both tables | 'left_only' = in batting but not salaries | 'right_only' = vice versa
print(bat_sal['_merge'].value_counts())
print(f'\nMatch rate: {(bat_sal["_merge"] == "both").mean():.1%}')



11.8.3 Step 3: Understand unmatched rows

unmatched = bat_sal[bat_sal['_merge'] == 'left_only']
print('Unmatched year range:', unmatched['yearID'].min(), '–', unmatched['yearID'].max())

The Lahman salary data begins in 1985. All seasons before that have no salary information — this is a structural gap in the data, not an error in the merge. Understanding why rows do not match is as important as knowing that they do not match.


11.8.4 Adding biographical data

bio_cols = ['playerID', 'nameFirst', 'nameLast', 'birthYear', 'birthState', 'birthCountry']
people_slim = people[bio_cols].copy()

bat_sal_bio = pd.merge(bat_sal, people_slim, on='playerID', how='left')

print('Shape after adding bio:', bat_sal_bio.shape)
print('Missing nameFirst:', bat_sal_bio['nameFirst'].isna().sum())

title: 11  Reshaping Data with pandas
text:
11.9 Applied Analysis: Salary and Performance
With a merged dataset, you can answer questions that required data from multiple tables.

# Filter to salary era with meaningful at-bats
qualified = bat_sal_bio[
    (bat_sal_bio['yearID'] >= 1985) &
    (bat_sal_bio['AB'] >= 200) &
    (bat_sal_bio['salary'].notna())
].copy()

qualified['sal_decile'] = pd.qcut(
    qualified['salary'], q=10,
    labels=['D1 (Lowest)', 'D2', 'D3', 'D4', 'D5',
            'D6', 'D7', 'D8', 'D9', 'D10 (Highest)']
)

perf_by_salary = (
    qualified
    .groupby('sal_decile', observed=True)[['BA', 'SLG']]
    .agg('mean')
    .round(3)
)

print('Mean batting stats by salary decile:')
perf_by_salary

title: 11  Reshaping Data with pandas
section: 11.10 MultiIndex: When Groups Become the Index
text:
11.10 MultiIndex: When Groups Become the Index
After groupby().agg() on multiple columns, pandas returns a MultiIndex — a hierarchical index that can be confusing if you are not expecting it. The most common solution is to call .reset_index() immediately to flatten it back to regular columns:

# Without reset_index — produces a MultiIndex
team_stats = (
    batting_modern
    .groupby(['decade', 'teamID'])[['HR', 'BA']]
    .agg(['mean', 'sum'])
)

print('MultiIndex columns:', team_stats.columns[:4])


# Flatten MultiIndex columns: join each (metric, agg_func) tuple with '_'
# e.g., ('HR', 'mean') → 'HR_mean', ('BA', 'sum') → 'BA_sum'
team_stats.columns = ['_'.join(col) for col in team_stats.columns]
# reset_index() moves the groupby keys (decade, teamID) from index back into columns
team_stats = team_stats.reset_index()
team_stats.head()

title: 11  Reshaping Data with pandas
text:
11.11 Common Issues in This Chapter






WarningMerge returned more rows than expected (“row inflation”)



What it means: You merged on keys that were not unique in one or both tables. A many-to-many merge explodes: 3 batting rows × 2 salary rows for the same player-year = 6 output rows.
How to fix it: Before merging, run df.groupby(key_cols).size().max() on each table. If it is > 1, aggregate to one row per key first. If you genuinely need the many-to-many, document why.








WarningMerge silently dropped rows (low match rate)



What it means: One of: (1) column-name mismatch — County vs county, (2) dtype mismatch — "2020" vs 2020, (3) whitespace or case — "Adams" vs "Adams " vs "adams", or (4) a legitimate structural gap (salaries start in 1985).
How to fix it: Always use indicator=True. Inspect left_only rows — what years, what keys, what values? Decide whether the gap is legitimate or a bug in your cleaning. Fix the keys upstream if it is a bug.








Warningconcat produced unexpected NaN columns



What it means: One DataFrame has "County" and another has "county". Concatenation aligns by column name, so each DataFrame contributes its own column and the other rows are NaN.
How to fix it: Standardize column names before concatenation: df.columns = df.columns.str.strip().str.lower(). Compare set(df1.columns) ^ set(df2.columns) to see mismatches.








Warningpivot raises ValueError: Index contains duplicate entries



What it means: pivot() does not aggregate and cannot pick between competing values. Your source has more than one row per (index, columns) pair.
How to fix it: Use pivot_table() with an aggfunc, or aggregate first with groupby().agg(). See Chapter 10 for the three-tools comparison.

title: 11  Reshaping Data with pandas
text:
11.12 Key Takeaways

Reshaping operations are both powerful and dangerous. Audit row counts and column names after every one.
Aggregate before you merge when the merge key is not unique. Fix row inflation upstream, not downstream.
Use indicator=True on every merge where correctness matters. The _merge column is your quality report.
Long format is usually easier for computation; wide format is usually easier for human reading. Know how to convert either direction.
concat stacks rows; merge combines tables on keys. Choose the right tool based on what question you are asking.

title: 11  Reshaping Data with pandas
text:
11.13 Further Reading






Tip📚 Going deeper on reshaping and merging






pandas: Merge, join, concatenate and compare — pandas.pydata.org/docs/user_guide/merging.html is the canonical reference on combining tables.
pandas: Reshaping and pivot tables — pandas.pydata.org/docs/user_guide/reshaping.html covers melt, pivot, pivot_table, stack, unstack.
Hadley Wickham: Tidy Data — vita.had.co.nz/papers/tidy-data.pdf is the foundational paper on long-format (“tidy”) data, written for R but conceptually identical in pandas.

title: 11  Reshaping Data with pandas
text:
11.14 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: Melt and validate





Take the wide dependency ratio table from Chapter 10 (counties as rows, age bands as columns). Melt it back to long format. Verify that the population totals match between the wide and long versions.






NoteSolution






age_band_cols = ['Children (0-17)', 'Working Age (18-64)', 'Seniors (65+)']

long_2020 = wide_2020.melt(
    id_vars=['county'],
    value_vars=age_band_cols,
    var_name='age_band',
    value_name='pop'
)

print('Wide sum:', wide_2020[age_band_cols].sum().sum())
print('Long sum:', long_2020['pop'].sum())













NoteExercise 2: Merge with audit





Merge the Lahman Teams.csv table onto the batting data using teamID and yearID as keys. Use indicator=True and report the match rate. How many batting rows have no corresponding team record?






NoteSolution






teams = pd.read_csv('Teams.csv')

bat_team = pd.merge(
    batting,
    teams[['teamID', 'yearID', 'name', 'W', 'L']],
    on=['teamID', 'yearID'],
    how='left',
    indicator=True
)

print(bat_team['_merge'].value_counts())
print(f'\nMatch rate: {(bat_team["_merge"] == "both").mean():.1%}')













NoteExercise 3: Colorado-born player analysis





Using the merged bat_sal_bio DataFrame, find all player-seasons where birthState == 'CO' and AB >= 100. Who are the top 10 Colorado-born players by career OPS?

title: 12  Loading Data with SQL
text:
12.1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:

title: 12  Loading Data with SQL
text:
Check that SQLite is installed and create a new .db database file from the terminal.
Distinguish SQLite dot-commands from SQL statements, and use .mode, .headers, and .import correctly.
Write a CREATE TABLE schema and load a clean CSV into the table.
Connect to a SQLite database from Python using sqlite3.connect and read query results into a pandas DataFrame with pd.read_sql_query.
Write exploratory SQL using SELECT, FROM, WHERE, GROUP BY, ORDER BY, and LIMIT, and translate between SQL and pandas equivalents.

title: 12  Loading Data with SQL
text:
12.2 Why SQL
This chapter marks the beginning of a new tool — SQL and the SQLite database engine — applied to the same Load–Clean–Extend–Reshape workflow you already know from pandas. Many of the concepts are identical: you are still loading data from files, inspecting it for problems, checking types and counts, and verifying that nothing went wrong. What changes is the interface: instead of calling Python methods on a DataFrame, you write declarative queries that describe what you want, and the database engine figures out how to get it.
SQL (Structured Query Language) has been the standard language for working with databases since the 1970s. You will encounter it in virtually every data-related career. SQLite is a lightweight database engine that stores an entire database in a single file on disk — no server, no installation wizard, no network configuration. This makes it ideal for learning SQL and for working with datasets that fit on a single machine.
The workflow in this chapter moves through four stages: orienting to the terminal, creating a database and schema in the SQLite shell, connecting to the database from Python, and running diagnostic queries with pd.read_sql_query().
You already know the Load–Clean–Extend–Reshape rhythm from Part II. The goal of this part is translation, not starting over. Most of your intuitions about inspecting data, validating transformations, and documenting decisions carry over — only the syntax changes.

title: 12  Loading Data with SQL
text:
12.3 Terminal Orientation
Before touching SQLite, you need three terminal commands. If you completed Chapter 1, these are review.



Command (Mac/Linux)
Command (Windows)
Purpose




pwd
cd (no arguments)
Print the current directory


ls
dir
List files in the current directory


cd folder
cd folder
Move into a folder




12.3.1 Checking SQLite installation
sqlite3 --version
If you see a version number (e.g., 3.43.2), SQLite is installed and on your PATH. If you get command not found, see Chapter 4 for installation instructions.

title: 12  Loading Data with SQL
text:
12.4 The SQLite Shell
The SQLite shell (sqlite3) is a command-line interface for interacting with a database directly. Think of it as a text-based spreadsheet viewer that also lets you define tables and run queries.

12.4.1 Dot-commands vs. SQL statements
The SQLite shell has two distinct types of commands. Dot-commands start with a period, do not end with a semicolon, and control the shell itself (.tables, .schema, .import, .quit). SQL statements are standard SQL, always end with a semicolon, and operate on data (SELECT ...;, CREATE TABLE ...;).
A SQL statement without a semicolon will sit and wait — SQLite thinks you are still typing. Adding a semicolon after a dot-command will produce an error. Getting these conventions straight early saves considerable frustration.


12.4.2 Step 1: Create the database file
Running sqlite3 cannabis.db either opens an existing file or creates a new one:
sqlite3 cannabis.db ".tables"
The file is created on disk when you first write data to it.


12.4.3 Step 2: Prepare the CSV






ImportantSQL builds on Python



The SQL workflow in this chapter depends on the Python workflow from Chapter 8 and Chapter 9. SQLite’s .import command requires a clean, rectangular CSV — exactly one header row, no aggregate rows, no currency formatting, no merged cells. Real-world government files rarely meet these requirements out of the box. The standard approach is to use pandas to load and clean the raw file first, then export a normalized CSV for SQLite to consume. If you have not completed Chapter 8 and Chapter 9, do so before continuing here.


SQLite’s .import command expects a clean, rectangular CSV with exactly one header row and no aggregate rows. The standard approach is to clean the file in pandas first — applying the same operations from Chapter 9 — and export a clean CSV for SQLite to consume.






NotePublic Interest Context: Enclosure by Interface



The fact that you must clean the cannabis file in pandas before SQLite can import it is not a coincidence. Government datasets are typically published through web portals designed for human download — one file at a time, formatted for spreadsheet users. The data is technically open, but the publishing workflow is optimized for a different kind of user than you.
This is a specific form of enclosure: access is possible, but it requires enough technical skill to navigate the formatting. The more agencies publish machine-readable APIs or consistently formatted CSV files with documented schemas, the more equitably public data can be used. See Chapter 7 for the broader framework.


This chapter uses pandas, numpy, and Python’s built-in sqlite3 module.






WarningIf you get an ImportError





sqlite3 ships with Python’s standard library — if you get ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'sqlite3', your Python install is unusual (this is very rare). For pandas or numpy, run conda install pandas numpy or pip install pandas numpy and restart the kernel. See Chapter 4.




import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

df_raw = pd.read_csv(
    'colorado_cannabis_sales_by_county.csv',
    header=2,        # row index 2 (zero-based) holds the real column headers
    skipfooter=8,    # skip the 8 footnote rows at the bottom of the file
    engine='python', # required when using skipfooter (the C parser doesn't support it)
    na_values=['NR'] # treat "NR" (Not Reported) as a missing value
)

# Rename to clean, SQL-friendly column names (lowercase, no spaces or special chars)
df_raw.columns = ['month', 'year', 'county', 'medical_sales', 'retail_sales']

# Strip currency formatting and cast to nullable integer
for col in ['medical_sales', 'retail_sales']:
    df_raw[col] = (
        df_raw[col]
        .str.strip()                           # remove any surrounding whitespace
        .str.replace('$', '', regex=False)     # remove dollar signs
        .str.replace(',', '', regex=False)     # remove thousands-separator commas
        .pipe(pd.to_numeric, errors='coerce')  # convert strings to numbers; 'coerce' turns failures to NaN
        .astype('Int64')                       # pandas nullable integer type (capital I) — supports NaN
    )                                          # regular int64 does NOT support NaN; Int64 does

# Remove statewide aggregate rows (county = 'Total'); these are not county-level data
df_raw = df_raw[df_raw['county'].str.strip() != 'Total']

# Export to a clean rectangular CSV — one header row, no special formatting
df_raw.to_csv('cannabis_clean.csv', index=False)  # index=False omits the row numbers column
print(f"Exported {len(df_raw):,} rows to cannabis_clean.csv")







TipSQL naming rules



Use lowercase letters, underscores, and no spaces or special characters for column names. The names in the CSV must exactly match the names in the CREATE TABLE schema you will define next. A mismatch causes the header row to be inserted as a data row — one of the most common SQLite loading errors.




12.4.4 Step 3: Define the schema
Before loading data, you declare the table structure — column names and their storage types:
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS cannabis_sales (
    month INTEGER,
    year INTEGER,
    county TEXT,
    medical_sales INTEGER,
    retail_sales INTEGER
);
SQLite has five storage classes: INTEGER, REAL, TEXT, BLOB, and NULL. For most work, you will use INTEGER, REAL, and TEXT.


12.4.5 Step 4: Load the CSV
The .import command reads a delimited file and inserts rows into a table. Always set .mode csv and .headers on before importing:
sqlite3 cannabis.db << 'EOF'
.mode csv
.headers on
.import cannabis_clean.csv cannabis_sales
EOF






WarningThe heredoc syntax



The << 'EOF' syntax sends multiple lines of input to SQLite through stdin. This is necessary because dot-commands cannot be passed as quoted string arguments on the command line. If you are on Windows, use the Python-side approach described in the next section instead.




12.4.6 Step 5: Post-load diagnostics
Run three checks immediately after loading:
# 1. Row count
sqlite3 cannabis.db "SELECT COUNT(*) AS row_count FROM cannabis_sales;"

# 2. First rows
sqlite3 -column -header cannabis.db "SELECT * FROM cannabis_sales LIMIT 5;"

# 3. Check for accidentally imported header row
sqlite3 cannabis.db "SELECT COUNT(*) AS header_rows FROM cannabis_sales WHERE county = 'county';"
If the header row count is greater than zero, delete it:
DELETE FROM cannabis_sales WHERE county = 'county';

title: 12  Loading Data with SQL
text:
12.5 Connecting from Python
The terminal workflow above is useful for setup and debugging, but your analysis work will happen in Jupyter notebooks using Python’s built-in sqlite3 module and pandas’ pd.read_sql_query().

import sqlite3

conn = sqlite3.connect('cannabis.db')
print("Connection established:", conn)







Warning



If the path is wrong, sqlite3.connect() will silently create a new empty file rather than raising an error. If your queries return “no such table,” check that you are pointing at the right file.



12.5.1 Low-level access with a cursor
A cursor is an object that manages the state of a single database operation — it tracks where you are in the result set, similar to a bookmark in a book. You execute() a query through the cursor, then use fetchone() to retrieve one row at a time, or fetchall() to retrieve all rows as a list of tuples. For most analytical work, pd.read_sql_query() is more convenient because it returns a DataFrame directly.

cursor = conn.cursor()
cursor.execute("SELECT COUNT(*) FROM cannabis_sales;")  # run the query
result = cursor.fetchone()  # retrieve the first (and only) row of results as a tuple
print("Row count:", result[0])  # result is a tuple like (8436,); [0] gets the first element



12.5.2 The preferred workflow: pd.read_sql_query()
This function sends a SQL query and returns a DataFrame — exactly like pd.read_csv(), but reading from a database instead of a file:

df = pd.read_sql_query("SELECT * FROM cannabis_sales LIMIT 10;", conn)
df

The result is a real pandas DataFrame. All the methods you learned in previous chapters — .shape, .dtypes, .describe(), .value_counts() — work exactly the same way.

title: 12  Loading Data with SQL
text:
12.6 SQL Syntax Fundamentals
Every query you write follows a standard clause structure. The clauses must appear in this order:
SELECT   county, year, retail_sales      -- 1. Which columns to return
FROM     cannabis_sales                  -- 2. Which table to read from
WHERE    year = 2023                     -- 3. Which rows to keep (filter)
GROUP BY county                          -- 4. How to collapse rows into groups
HAVING   SUM(retail_sales) > 1000000     -- 5. Filter on group-level results
ORDER BY retail_sales DESC               -- 6. How to sort the output
LIMIT    10;                             -- 7. Maximum rows to return
Not every clause is required. A minimal query needs only SELECT and FROM:
SELECT * FROM cannabis_sales;

12.6.1 Keywords and case
SQL is case-insensitive for keywords — SELECT, select, and Select all work. The convention is to capitalize SQL keywords and use lowercase for table names, column names, and aliases. This helps your eye parse the clause structure quickly.


12.6.2 Literals
When you write a value directly in a query, always use single quotes for text strings ('Boulder'), no quotes for numbers (2023), and NULL for missing values. Double quotes in SQLite are for identifiers (column names), not string values.


12.6.3 The NULL gotcha
NULL = NULL evaluates to NULL, not TRUE. You cannot use = to test for missing values. Always use IS NULL or IS NOT NULL.

title: 12  Loading Data with SQL
text:
12.7 Exploratory Queries
With the data loaded and a Python connection established, apply the post-load checklist using SQL:

# Shape equivalent
pd.read_sql_query("SELECT COUNT(*) AS n_rows FROM cannabis_sales;", conn)


# Head equivalent
pd.read_sql_query("SELECT * FROM cannabis_sales LIMIT 5;", conn)


# Value counts equivalent
pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT county, COUNT(*) AS n_months
    FROM cannabis_sales
    GROUP BY county
    ORDER BY n_months DESC
    LIMIT 10;
""", conn)


# Describe equivalent for a numeric column
pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT
        MIN(retail_sales) AS min_val,
        MAX(retail_sales) AS max_val,
        AVG(retail_sales) AS mean_val,
        COUNT(*) AS n_rows,
        SUM(CASE WHEN retail_sales IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS n_nulls
    FROM cannabis_sales;
""", conn)

title: 12  Loading Data with SQL
12  Loading Data with SQL
text:
12.9 Common Issues in This Chapter






WarningError: near "<dot-command>": syntax error



What it means: You tried to run a dot-command ending with a semicolon, or you tried to run dot-commands inside a .sql file.
How to fix it: Dot-commands (like .tables, .mode csv, .import) are shell commands — no semicolon. SQL statements (like SELECT ...;) always end with a semicolon. They belong in different places.








WarningError: no such table: cannabis_sales



What it means: Your connection is pointing at the wrong database file (often a brand-new empty one created accidentally). sqlite3.connect('cannabis.db') silently creates an empty file if cannabis.db is not in the current directory.
How to fix it: Run print(os.getcwd()) in your notebook. Confirm cannabis.db is in that directory. Use an absolute path if necessary. Verify the file has tables with sqlite3 cannabis.db ".tables" from the terminal.








WarningThe header row got imported as data



What it means: You forgot .headers on before .import, so SQLite treated the first row as data.
How to fix it: Run DELETE FROM <table> WHERE <text_col> = '<header_name>'; to remove the stray row. Then rebuild the workflow with .headers on set before .import.








WarningA numeric column contains NULL where you expected numbers



What it means: SQLite does not enforce storage classes strictly — a TEXT value in an INTEGER column is stored as text unless you pre-cleaned it. If you loaded a CSV with $1,234 still present, the column is a mix of text and numbers.
How to fix it: Clean the CSV in pandas before importing. .describe() in pandas and SELECT MIN(...), MAX(...), COUNT(...) in SQL will reveal the issue. See Chapter 9.

title: 12  Loading Data with SQL
text:
12.10 Key Takeaways

SQLite is a file-based database: your whole database is one .db file. No server, no setup wizard.
Always clean CSVs in pandas before .import. SQLite expects rectangular, single-header-row data.
Dot-commands control the shell; SQL statements operate on data. Know which is which.
pd.read_sql_query(sql, conn) is the bridge — it returns a pandas DataFrame, so every pandas skill you built in Part II still applies.
The SQL query structure (SELECT ... FROM ... WHERE ... GROUP BY ... HAVING ... ORDER BY ... LIMIT) appears in a fixed order. Memorize the order; the clauses inside each are flexible.

text:

SQLite documentation — sqlite.org/docs.html is the canonical reference. Start with “SQL syntax” and “Command-line shell.”

12.12 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: Create and load a second table





Clean the Colorado population data in pandas, export it as a CSV, define a schema for a population table, and load it into the same database. Run the three post-load diagnostic checks.

pop = pop[pop['datatype'] == 'Estimate']
pop.to_csv('population_clean.csv', index=False)

CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS population (
    countyfips TEXT,
    county TEXT,
    year INTEGER,

);
Then import using .mode csv, .headers on, and .import population_clean.csv population.












NoteExercise 2: Diagnostic queries





Write SQL queries to answer: (a) How many distinct counties are in the cannabis_sales table? (b) What is the year range? (c) How many rows have NULL retail_sales? (d) Which county has the most months of data?






NoteSolution






queries = [
    "SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT county) AS n_counties FROM cannabis_sales;",
    "SELECT MIN(year) AS min_year, MAX(year) AS max_year FROM cannabis_sales;",
    "SELECT COUNT(*) AS null_retail FROM cannabis_sales WHERE retail_sales IS NULL;",
    """SELECT county, COUNT(*) AS n_months FROM cannabis_sales
       GROUP BY county ORDER BY n_months DESC LIMIT 5;"""
]

for q in queries:
    print(pd.read_sql_query(q, conn))
    print()













NoteExercise 3: Load and query a second table





Prepare the Colorado State Demography Office population data for SQLite:

Load sya-county.csv in pandas using skiprows=1 and filter to Estimate rows only.
Export it as population_clean.csv.
Define a schema for a population table in SQLite (at minimum: countyfips TEXT, county TEXT, year INTEGER, age INTEGER, totalpopulation INTEGER).
Load the CSV into your cannabis.db database using the .import command.
Write a SQL query that counts the number of distinct counties and years in the table, and verify the row count matches what pandas reported.







NoteSolution






# Step 1–2: Prepare and export
pop = pd.read_csv('sya-county.csv', skiprows=1, dtype={'countyfips': str})
pop = pop[pop['datatype'] == 'Estimate']
pop.to_csv('population_clean.csv', index=False)
print(f"Exported {len(pop):,} rows")

-- Step 3: Schema
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS population (
    countyfips TEXT,
    county TEXT,
    year INTEGER,
    age INTEGER,
    malepopulation INTEGER,
    femalepopulation INTEGER,
    totalpopulation INTEGER,
    datatype TEXT
);
# Step 5: Verify
pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT
        COUNT(DISTINCT county) AS n_counties,
        COUNT(DISTINCT year) AS n_years,
        COUNT(*) AS total_rows
    FROM population;
""", conn)

title: 13  Cleaning Data with SQL
text:
13.1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:

title: 13  Cleaning Data with SQL
text:
Apply a five-stage Inspect → Clean → Design Schema → Load → Validate workflow to a messy dataset.
Write SQL diagnostic queries for missingness, duplicates, inconsistent categories, and invalid ranges.
Use SQLite’s built-in string functions (TRIM, UPPER, LOWER, REPLACE) and CASE WHEN expressions to clean columns.
Diagnose and repair silent join failures caused by inconsistent key formatting across agency sources.
Consolidate cleaned tables from multiple source databases into a single analysis database using DataFrame.to_sql.

title: 13  Cleaning Data with SQL
text:
13.2 Why SQL for Cleaning
Cleaning data in SQL follows the same logic you learned in pandas — identify missingness, duplicates, inconsistent categories, and invalid ranges — but uses a different set of tools. SQL excels at diagnostic queries: you can characterize problems across an entire table with a single statement, and the declarative syntax makes your logic explicit and auditable. This chapter applies a structured diagnostic framework to three real Colorado government datasets, loads them into a SQLite database, and demonstrates a critical lesson about silent join failures caused by inconsistent key formatting across sources.
The silent join failure in this chapter is the clearest example in the book of a bug that does not look like a bug. A query runs, returns results, and those results are systematically wrong. Reading this chapter carefully will save you days of confusion in your future data work.

title: 13  Cleaning Data with SQL
text:
13.3 The Diagnostic Framework
Every cleaning session in this chapter follows the same five stages:
Inspect → Clean → Design Schema → Load → Validate
Within the Inspect stage, we run four standard diagnostics, one per category of data quality issue:

Missingness — which columns have nulls, and how many?
Duplicates — are there repeated rows on the expected key columns?
Inconsistent categories — do text columns have variant spellings, extra whitespace, or unexpected values?
Invalid ranges — are numeric and date columns within plausible bounds?

This is the same INSPECT → TRANSFORM → VALIDATE → DOCUMENT workflow from the pandas chapters, specialized for SQL.

title: 13  Cleaning Data with SQL
text:
13.4 Setup
This chapter uses Python’s built-in sqlite3 module, pandas, and numpy.

import sqlite3
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

pd.set_option('display.max_columns', 20)
pd.set_option('display.max_colwidth', 55)

conn = sqlite3.connect('colorado_public_health.db')
cursor = conn.cursor()







WarningIf you get an ImportError





sqlite3 is part of the Python standard library. For pandas or numpy: conda install pandas numpy or pip install pandas numpy. Restart your Jupyter kernel after installing. See Chapter 4.

text:
13.5 Datasets
This chapter works with three Colorado government datasets that will be joined together in Chapter 15.








File
Source
Description




cdc_deaths.csv
CDC WONDER
Colorado mortality counts by county, year, age group, and ICD-10 chapter

title: 13  Cleaning Data with SQL
text:
13.6 Part 1: CDC Mortality Data

13.6.1 Inspect
Load the raw file in pandas and run each diagnostic.

deaths_raw = pd.read_csv('cdc_deaths.csv')
print('Shape:', deaths_raw.shape)
deaths_raw.head()


13.6.1.1 Missingness

print(deaths_raw.isna().sum())



13.6.1.2 Duplicates

key_cols = ['County', 'Year', 'Age Group', 'ICD Chapter']
print('Duplicates on key:', deaths_raw.duplicated(subset=key_cols).sum())



13.6.1.3 Inconsistent categories

print(deaths_raw['County'].value_counts().head(10))

Pay attention to the format of county names. Does this dataset use 'Adams', 'Adams County', or 'Adams County, CO'? This will matter critically when we join tables later.


13.6.1.4 Invalid ranges

print('Year range:', deaths_raw['Year'].min(), '–', deaths_raw['Year'].max())
print('Deaths range:', deaths_raw['Deaths'].min(), '–', deaths_raw['Deaths'].max())




13.6.2 Clean
Apply cleaning operations in pandas based on what the diagnostics revealed. Strip whitespace from text columns, standardize county names, and handle any sentinel values (CDC WONDER uses “Suppressed” and “Unreliable” as data quality flags).

deaths_clean = deaths_raw.copy()

# Strip whitespace from all text columns
for col in deaths_clean.select_dtypes(include='object').columns:
    deaths_clean[col] = deaths_clean[col].str.strip()



13.6.3 Design Schema
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS deaths (
    county TEXT,
    year INTEGER,
    age_group TEXT,
    icd_chapter TEXT,
    deaths INTEGER
);


13.6.4 Load

deaths_clean.to_csv('deaths_clean.csv', index=False)



13.6.5 Validate
After loading into SQLite, run diagnostic queries to confirm the import matches the pandas source:

pd.read_sql_query("SELECT COUNT(*) AS n_rows FROM deaths;", conn)


pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT county, COUNT(*) AS n
    FROM deaths
    GROUP BY county
    ORDER BY n DESC
    LIMIT 5;
""", conn)

Cross-check the SQL count against the pandas count — they should match exactly.

title: 13  Cleaning Data with SQL
text:
13.7 Part 2: CBI Crime Data
The same five-stage process applies. The crime data introduces additional challenges: jurisdiction names that do not match county names, nullable columns with complex patterns, and timestamp fields that require careful parsing.

13.7.1 Inspect

crime_raw = pd.read_csv('cbi_crime.csv')
print('Shape:', crime_raw.shape)
crime_raw.head()


13.7.1.1 Missingness

print(crime_raw.isna().sum())



13.7.1.2 Duplicates

# The expected key depends on whether each row is one offense or one report.
# Check the grain: how many rows share the same (jurisdiction, offense_date, offense_type)?
key_cols = ['jurisdiction', 'offense_date', 'offense_type']
print('Duplicates on key:', crime_raw.duplicated(subset=key_cols).sum())



13.7.1.3 Inconsistent categories

# Check jurisdiction names — these do not always map cleanly to county names
print(crime_raw['jurisdiction'].value_counts().head(15))


# The 'county' column may differ from 'jurisdiction' — check both
print(crime_raw['county'].value_counts().head(10))



13.7.1.4 Invalid ranges

# Check date column — is it a parseable date string?
print('offense_date sample:', crime_raw['offense_date'].dropna().head(5).tolist())




13.7.2 Clean

crime_clean = crime_raw.copy()

# Strip whitespace from all string columns
for col in crime_clean.select_dtypes(include='object').columns:
    crime_clean[col] = crime_clean[col].str.strip()



13.7.3 Design Schema
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS crime (
    jurisdiction TEXT,
    offense_date TEXT,
    offense_type TEXT,
    county TEXT
);


13.7.4 Load

crime_clean.to_csv('crime_clean.csv', index=False)



13.7.5 Validate

pd.read_sql_query("SELECT COUNT(*) AS n_rows FROM crime;", conn)


pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT county, COUNT(*) AS n
    FROM crime
    GROUP BY county
    ORDER BY n DESC
    LIMIT 5;
""", conn)

title: 13  Cleaning Data with SQL
text:
13.8 Part 3: Population Estimates

13.8.1 Inspect

pop_raw = pd.read_csv('sya-county.csv', skiprows=1, dtype={'countyfips': str})
# Filter to historical estimates only; 'Forecast' rows are projections, not measurements
pop_raw = pop_raw[pop_raw['datatype'] == 'Estimate'].copy()
print('Shape:', pop_raw.shape)
pop_raw.head()

The population data has its own quirks: a header row that needs to be skipped, a datatype column that mixes estimates and forecasts, and county names in yet another format.

13.8.1.1 Missingness

print(pop_raw.isna().sum())



13.8.1.2 Duplicates

# Each row should be unique on (county, year, age)
key_cols = ['county', 'year', 'age']
print('Duplicates on (county, year, age):', pop_raw.duplicated(subset=key_cols).sum())



13.8.1.3 Inconsistent categories

# Note the county name format — bare names like 'Adams', not 'Adams County'
print(pop_raw['county'].value_counts().head(10))



13.8.1.4 Invalid ranges

print('Year range:', pop_raw['year'].min(), '–', pop_raw['year'].max())
print('Age range:', pop_raw['age'].min(), '–', pop_raw['age'].max())
print('Total population sample:')
print(pop_raw['totalpopulation'].describe())




13.8.2 Clean

pop_clean = pop_raw.copy()

# Strip any whitespace from text columns
for col in pop_clean.select_dtypes(include='object').columns:
    pop_clean[col] = pop_clean[col].str.strip()



13.8.3 Design Schema
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS population (
    countyfips TEXT,
    county TEXT,
    year INTEGER,
    age INTEGER,
    malepopulation INTEGER,
    femalepopulation INTEGER,
    totalpopulation INTEGER,
    datatype TEXT
);


13.8.4 Load

pop_clean.to_csv('population_clean.csv', index=False)



13.8.5 Validate

pd.read_sql_query("SELECT COUNT(*) AS n_rows FROM population;", conn)


# Cross-check: the SQL count should match the pandas count
print('pandas count:', len(pop_clean))

title: 13  Cleaning Data with SQL
text:
13.9 Part 4: The Silent Join Failure
This is the most important section of the chapter. After loading all three tables, we discover that county names are formatted differently in each source:



Table
Example county name




deaths
Adams County, CO


crime
Adams County


population
Adams



What happens when you try to join these tables on county name? Nothing matches. The join produces zero rows — silently, with no error message. This is a silent join failure, and it is one of the most dangerous problems in data work because it looks like the query succeeded (it ran without errors) but the result is empty or incomplete.

13.9.1 Demonstrating the failure

pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT d.county AS death_county, p.county AS pop_county, COUNT(*) AS n
    FROM deaths d
    JOIN population p ON d.county = p.county
    LIMIT 5;
""", conn)

If this returns zero rows, the county formats do not match.


13.9.2 Diagnosing the mismatch

pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT DISTINCT county FROM deaths ORDER BY county LIMIT 10;
""", conn)


pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT DISTINCT county FROM population ORDER BY county LIMIT 10;
""", conn)



13.9.3 Fixing the mismatch
The fix is to standardize county names to a common format before loading, or to use SQL string functions to normalize them at query time. The REPLACE() and TRIM() functions in SQLite can help, but the cleanest approach is to fix the data in pandas before loading:

# Standardize all county names to bare names: 'Adams'
# From 'Adams County, CO' → remove ', CO' then remove ' County'
# From 'Adams County' → remove ' County'
# From 'Adams' → already correct



13.9.4 The lesson
County name mismatches across sources are not a quirk of these particular datasets — they are a universal pattern. Any time you join data from different sources on a text key, you must verify that the key values actually match before running the join. The diagnostic pattern is: list the distinct values from each table, compare them, and fix any mismatches before joining.






NotePublic Interest Context: Erosion



The three county name formats — "Adams County, CO", "Adams County", "Adams" — come from three Colorado government agencies that built their data systems independently, at different times, with no shared naming authority. No one decided to make them incompatible. The mismatch is the accumulated result of decades of decentralized infrastructure development.
This is infrastructure erosion: not a sudden break, but a gradual divergence that only becomes visible when you try to connect the systems. The cost is borne by anyone who wants to analyze these datasets together — which is exactly the analysis you would want to perform if you were studying public health outcomes across Colorado counties.
The standardization work you completed (removing “, CO” and ” County”) is a technical fix for a governance problem. The fix lives in your notebook; the problem persists in the source systems. See Chapter 7 for the broader framework.

title: 13  Cleaning Data with SQL
text:
13.10 SQL Functions for Cleaning
Several SQL functions are useful for cleaning operations:








Function
Purpose
Example




TRIM(col)
Remove leading/trailing whitespace
SELECT TRIM(county) FROM deaths;


UPPER(col)
Convert to uppercase
SELECT UPPER(county) FROM deaths;


LOWER(col)
Convert to lowercase
SELECT LOWER(county) FROM deaths;


REPLACE(col, old, new)
Replace substring
SELECT REPLACE(county, ' County', '') FROM deaths;


CAST(col AS type)
Change data type
SELECT CAST(year AS INTEGER) FROM deaths;


COALESCE(col, default)
Replace NULL with default
SELECT COALESCE(deaths, 0) FROM deaths;


CASE WHEN
Conditional logic
See below




13.10.1 CASE WHEN for conditional cleaning
SELECT
    county,
    CASE
        WHEN deaths IS NULL THEN 'Missing'
        WHEN deaths = 0 THEN 'Zero'
        WHEN deaths < 10 THEN 'Suppressed range'
        ELSE 'Normal'
    END AS death_status
FROM deaths;

title: 13  Cleaning Data with SQL
13  Cleaning Data with SQL
text:
13.12 Saving the Cleaned Tables
After validating each dataset, collect the cleaned, analysis-ready versions into a single SQLite database for use in Chapter 15. That chapter expects one deaths table, one crime table, and one population table — all in colorado_public_health.db, with county names standardized to bare county names (e.g., Adams) and all rows validated.
The cleanest approach is to read each table from its source database into a pandas DataFrame, apply the county-name standardization you diagnosed above, and write to the shared database using to_sql:

import pandas as pd
import sqlite3

# Read cleaned tables from their source databases
conn_deaths = sqlite3.connect('cdc_deaths.db')
conn_crime = sqlite3.connect('cbi_crime.db')
conn_pop = sqlite3.connect('population.db')

deaths = pd.read_sql_query("SELECT * FROM deaths", conn_deaths)
crime = pd.read_sql_query("SELECT * FROM crime", conn_crime)
population = pd.read_sql_query("SELECT * FROM population", conn_pop)

# Standardize county names to bare format (e.g., "Adams County, CO" -> "Adams")
deaths['county'] = (deaths['county']
    .str.replace(r',\s*CO$', '', regex=True)   # remove ", CO"
    .str.replace(r'\s+County$', '', regex=True) # remove " County"
    .str.strip()
)

crime['county'] = (crime['county']
    .str.replace(r'\s+County$', '', regex=True)
    .str.strip()
)
# population already uses bare county names

# Write to shared database
conn_ph = sqlite3.connect('colorado_public_health.db')
deaths.to_sql('deaths', conn_ph, if_exists='replace', index=False)
crime.to_sql('crime', conn_ph, if_exists='replace', index=False)
population.to_sql('population', conn_ph, if_exists='replace', index=False)

print("Tables written to colorado_public_health.db:")
for tbl in ['deaths', 'crime', 'population']:
    n = pd.read_sql_query(f"SELECT COUNT(*) AS n FROM {tbl}", conn_ph).iloc[0, 0]
    print(f"  {tbl}: {n:,} rows")

conn_deaths.close(); conn_crime.close(); conn_pop.close(); conn_ph.close()







TipWhy not write directly from SQL?



SQLite does not have a built-in cross-database INSERT INTO ... SELECT FROM for different .db files. The pandas round-trip (read into DataFrame, standardize, write back) is the practical approach for consolidating multiple source databases into one analysis database.

title: 13  Cleaning Data with SQL
text:
13.13 Common Issues in This Chapter






WarningA join on a text key returns zero rows



What it means: A silent join failure. Your keys look similar but are not identical — often "Adams" vs "Adams County" vs "Adams County, CO".
How to fix it: Run SELECT DISTINCT county FROM <table> ORDER BY county on each side of the join. Compare the results. Normalize in pandas (or with SQLite TRIM/REPLACE) before joining.








WarningTRIM does not catch some whitespace



What it means: Non-breaking spaces (Unicode U+00A0) and tab characters are whitespace to humans but not to SQLite’s default TRIM. A column can still fail to match after TRIM if the whitespace is unusual.
How to fix it: Check for unusual whitespace in pandas with df['col'].str.contains(r'\s', regex=True) and explicitly replace \xa0 and other characters before exporting. In SQL, REPLACE(col, char(160), ' ') handles non-breaking spaces.








WarningCAST(x AS INTEGER) returns 0 unexpectedly



What it means: SQLite’s CAST returns 0 for strings that cannot be parsed. A column with $1,234 gets stored as text, and CAST silently produces zeros.
How to fix it: Clean currency/comma characters before loading. In SQLite, chain CAST(REPLACE(REPLACE(col, '$', ''), ',', '') AS INTEGER) — but doing the cleanup in pandas first is clearer and easier to audit.








WarningNULL behaves unexpectedly in conditions



What it means: NULL = NULL is NULL (not TRUE), and NULL = 'Adams' is NULL. Any row with NULL in the compared column is silently excluded.
How to fix it: Always use IS NULL and IS NOT NULL for null checks. Use COALESCE(col, default) when you need a default value.

title: 13  Cleaning Data with SQL
text:
13.14 Key Takeaways

Cleaning in SQL follows the same Inspect → Clean → Validate → Document rhythm as pandas; only the syntax changes.
The four diagnostic categories — missingness, duplicates, inconsistent categories, invalid ranges — are worth running on every new table.
Silent join failures are the most dangerous SQL bug class. Always run SELECT DISTINCT on join keys before joining.
SQLite’s type system is permissive. Column names and types in your schema do not enforce what is actually stored — audit after every load.
When a technical fix (standardizing county names) encodes a governance problem (three agencies, no shared convention), document the fix in your Known Issues Log and the provenance in your Data Dictionary.

title: 13  Cleaning Data with SQL
text:
13.15 Further Reading






Tip📚 Going deeper on SQL cleaning and SQLite internals






SQLite: Core built-in functions — sqlite.org/lang_corefunc.html lists every built-in function (TRIM, UPPER, COALESCE, etc.).
SQLite: Datatypes in SQLite — sqlite.org/datatype3.html explains SQLite’s flexible (and sometimes surprising) type system.
SQLite: Expression reference — sqlite.org/lang_expr.html covers CASE WHEN, IS NULL, operators, and NULL semantics.
pandas: to_sql API — pandas.pydata.org/docs/reference/api/pandas.DataFrame.to_sql.html is the reference for writing DataFrames into SQLite.

title: 13  Cleaning Data with SQL
text:
13.16 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: Missingness by year





Write a SQL query that shows the number of NULL death counts per year in the deaths table. Which years have the most missing data?






NoteSolution






pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT year,
           COUNT(*) AS total_rows,
           SUM(CASE WHEN deaths IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS n_null
    FROM deaths
    GROUP BY year
    ORDER BY year;
""", conn)













NoteExercise 2: TRIM audit





Write a query that identifies rows in the crime table where the jurisdiction column has leading or trailing whitespace (i.e., jurisdiction <> TRIM(jurisdiction)). How many rows are affected?






NoteSolution






pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT COUNT(*) AS whitespace_rows
    FROM crime
    WHERE jurisdiction <> TRIM(jurisdiction);
""", conn)













NoteExercise 3: Cross-table county audit





Write queries that list the distinct county names from all three tables. Identify at least two counties whose names differ across sources. Propose a standardization strategy.

title: 14  Extending Data with SQL
text:
14.1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:

title: 14  Extending Data with SQL
text:
Write GROUP BY queries using SQLite’s aggregate functions (COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX, GROUP_CONCAT).
Distinguish WHERE (filters rows before grouping) from HAVING (filters groups after aggregation).
Use CASE WHEN expressions inside aggregates for conditional counting and cross-tabulation.
Build subqueries in SELECT, WHERE, and FROM clauses.
Write multi-step analytical pipelines using Common Table Expressions (CTEs) with the WITH ... AS syntax.

title: 14  Extending Data with SQL
text:
14.2 Why SQL for Extending
Loading and cleaning produce reliable tables. Extending transforms those tables into answers. This chapter introduces the SQL tools for computing metrics and testing analytical claims: GROUP BY with aggregate functions, subqueries that embed one query inside another, and Common Table Expressions (CTEs) that name intermediate results for clarity and reuse. Together, these tools let you write complex analytical pipelines entirely in SQL — pipelines that are readable, auditable, and reproducible.
The dataset for this chapter is the Boulder County Assessor’s buildings file, which contains one row per improvement section recorded for each parcel. It is a rich, messy, real-world dataset with dozens of columns describing building characteristics — year built, square footage, quality grade, heating system, roof type, and more.
CTEs are the single biggest readability upgrade you can make to your SQL. If a query is turning into a wall of nested parentheses, that is a signal to break it apart with WITH. Your reviewer — even your future self — will thank you.

title: 14  Extending Data with SQL
text:
14.3 Setup
This chapter uses Python’s built-in sqlite3, pandas, and numpy.

import sqlite3
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

pd.set_option('display.max_columns', 20)
pd.set_option('display.max_colwidth', 55)
pd.set_option('display.float_format', '{:.2f}'.format)







WarningIf you get an ImportError





sqlite3 is part of Python’s standard library. For pandas or numpy: conda install pandas numpy or pip install pandas numpy, then restart the Jupyter kernel. See Chapter 4.




# Load the CSV, clean, and write to a local SQLite database
buildings_raw = pd.read_csv('buildings.csv')

# Strip trailing whitespace from text columns
for col in buildings_raw.select_dtypes(include='object').columns:
    buildings_raw[col] = buildings_raw[col].str.strip()

# Write to database
conn = sqlite3.connect('boulder_buildings.db')
buildings_raw.to_sql('buildings', conn, if_exists='replace', index=False)

print(f"Loaded {len(buildings_raw):,} rows into buildings table")

title: 14  Extending Data with SQL
section: 14.4 Part 1: GROUP BY and Aggregate Functions
text:
14.4 Part 1: GROUP BY and Aggregate Functions

14.4.1 What GROUP BY does
GROUP BY collapses rows that share the same value(s) in one or more columns into a single summary row. It is the SQL equivalent of pandas’ groupby().agg() and the conceptual equivalent of a spreadsheet pivot table.
The key rule: every column in your SELECT must be either listed in GROUP BY or wrapped in an aggregate function. You cannot select a raw column alongside grouped aggregates — SQL would not know which row’s value to display.

pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT
        quality_grade,
        COUNT(*) AS n_buildings,
        AVG(above_ground_sqft) AS avg_sqft,
        MIN(year_built) AS oldest,
        MAX(year_built) AS newest
    FROM buildings
    WHERE quality_grade IS NOT NULL
    GROUP BY quality_grade
    ORDER BY n_buildings DESC;
""", conn)



14.4.2 SQLite aggregate functions








Function
Purpose
Notes




COUNT(*)
Number of rows in group
Counts all rows, including NULLs


COUNT(col)
Number of non-NULL values
Skips NULLs — use to measure completeness


SUM(col)
Sum of values
Returns NULL if all values are NULL


TOTAL(col)
Sum of values
Returns 0.0 if all values are NULL (safer)


AVG(col)
Mean of non-NULL values
Ignores NULLs — denominator is count of non-NULL


MIN(col) / MAX(col)
Extremes
Works on text (alphabetical) and numbers


GROUP_CONCAT(col)
Concatenate values into a string
Useful for inventory lists



The distinction between COUNT(*) and COUNT(col) is critical. COUNT(*) counts all rows regardless of NULL values. COUNT(col) counts only rows where col is not NULL. Comparing the two tells you the completeness rate of a column.

pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT
        COUNT(*) AS total_rows,            -- all rows, including NULLs
        COUNT(year_built) AS non_null_year, -- only rows where year_built is not NULL
        COUNT(*) - COUNT(year_built) AS null_year,  -- the difference = number of NULLs
        -- 100.0 forces floating-point division; integer / integer would round to 0 or 1
        ROUND(100.0 * COUNT(year_built) / COUNT(*), 1) AS pct_complete
    FROM buildings;
""", conn)



14.4.3 HAVING vs. WHERE
Both filter rows, but they operate at different stages of query execution. WHERE filters individual rows before grouping. HAVING filters groups after aggregation.

# WHERE: filter rows before grouping
pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT quality_grade, COUNT(*) AS n
    FROM buildings
    WHERE year_built >= 2000
    GROUP BY quality_grade
    ORDER BY n DESC;
""", conn)


# HAVING: filter groups after aggregation
pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT quality_grade, COUNT(*) AS n
    FROM buildings
    GROUP BY quality_grade
    HAVING n >= 100
    ORDER BY n DESC;
""", conn)

A useful mnemonic: WHERE answers “which rows go into the groups?” while HAVING answers “which groups appear in the output?”


14.4.4 CASE WHEN inside aggregation
CASE WHEN expressions inside aggregate functions enable conditional counting — the SQL equivalent of COUNTIF in a spreadsheet. This is one of the most powerful patterns in SQL.

# CASE WHEN inside SUM() is the SQL equivalent of COUNTIF in a spreadsheet:
# the CASE expression returns 1 when the condition is true, 0 otherwise.
# Summing these 1s and 0s counts how many rows satisfy each condition.
pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT
        (year_built / 10) * 10 AS decade,  -- integer division truncates, e.g. 1987/10=198, *10=1980
        SUM(CASE WHEN bldg_class = 'R' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS residential,
        SUM(CASE WHEN bldg_class = 'C' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS commercial,
        SUM(CASE WHEN bldg_class NOT IN ('R', 'C') THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS other,
        COUNT(*) AS total
    FROM buildings
    WHERE year_built IS NOT NULL
    GROUP BY decade
    ORDER BY decade;
""", conn)



14.4.5 Working with Dates in SQL: strftime()
Government datasets often store dates as separate integer columns rather than a single date column. The cannabis sales data stores month and year as separate integers. SQL can reconstruct a date string from these using strftime() and string concatenation.

# First, load the cleaned cannabis data if you have it
cannabis_conn = sqlite3.connect('boulder_buildings.db')  # reuse same connection

pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT
        strftime('%Y-Q', year || '-' || printf('%02d', month) || '-01') AS quarter,
        SUM(retail_sales) AS total_retail,
        COUNT(DISTINCT county) AS n_counties
    FROM cannabis_sales
    WHERE county != 'Total'
    GROUP BY quarter
    ORDER BY quarter
    LIMIT 12;
""", conn)

printf('%02d', month) left-pads the month integer with a zero so that 3 becomes 03. SQLite’s strftime() then accepts the reconstructed date string 'YYYY-MM-DD' and formats it however you need.






TipSQLite date functions are string-based



Unlike PostgreSQL or MySQL, SQLite has no native DATE type. Dates are stored as text, integer, or real values, and strftime() operates on text in ISO format (YYYY-MM-DD). As long as your date strings are in that format, strftime() gives you full access to year, month, quarter, day-of-week, and Julian day calculations.

title: 14  Extending Data with SQL
text:
14.5 Part 1 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: Construction era distribution





Write a query that groups buildings into construction eras — pre-1950, 1950–1979, 1980–1999, 2000–present — and counts the number of buildings in each era. Use CASE WHEN to create the era labels.






NoteSolution






pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT
        CASE
            WHEN year_built < 1950 THEN 'Pre-1950'
            WHEN year_built < 1980 THEN '1950-1979'
            WHEN year_built < 2000 THEN '1980-1999'
            ELSE '2000-present'
        END AS era,
        COUNT(*) AS n_buildings,
        ROUND(AVG(above_ground_sqft), 0) AS avg_sqft
    FROM buildings
    WHERE year_built IS NOT NULL
    GROUP BY era
    ORDER BY MIN(year_built);
""", conn)













NoteExercise 2: Heating system prevalence





Write a query that shows the top 10 heating system types by count, along with the average square footage and average year built for buildings with each heating type. Exclude rows where the heating system is NULL.






NoteSolution






pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT
        heating_type,
        COUNT(*) AS n,
        ROUND(AVG(above_ground_sqft), 0) AS avg_sqft,
        ROUND(AVG(year_built), 0) AS avg_year
    FROM buildings
    WHERE heating_type IS NOT NULL
    GROUP BY heating_type
    ORDER BY n DESC
    LIMIT 10;
""", conn)

title: 14  Extending Data with SQL
text:
14.6 Part 2: Subqueries
A subquery is a SELECT statement nested inside another query. It lets you compute a value or a set of values dynamically and use the result in a filter, comparison, or derived table — without hardcoding anything.

14.6.1 Scalar subquery: comparing each row to a reference value
A scalar subquery returns a single value. You can use it anywhere a literal value would go:

# Find buildings larger than the overall average
pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT strap, year_built, above_ground_sqft
    FROM buildings
    WHERE above_ground_sqft > (SELECT AVG(above_ground_sqft) FROM buildings)
    ORDER BY above_ground_sqft DESC
    LIMIT 10;
""", conn)

The subquery (SELECT AVG(above_ground_sqft) FROM buildings) computes the mean once, and the outer query uses it to filter rows. This is more robust than hardcoding the average — if the data changes, the query automatically adapts.


14.6.2 WHERE IN: filtering against a dynamically computed set

# Find buildings in quality grades that have above-average mean square footage
pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT strap, quality_grade, above_ground_sqft
    FROM buildings
    WHERE quality_grade IN (
        SELECT quality_grade
        FROM buildings
        WHERE quality_grade IS NOT NULL
        GROUP BY quality_grade
        HAVING AVG(above_ground_sqft) > (SELECT AVG(above_ground_sqft) FROM buildings)
    )
    ORDER BY above_ground_sqft DESC
    LIMIT 10;
""", conn)



14.6.3 Derived table: wrapping aggregate results
A subquery in the FROM clause creates a temporary table (called a derived table) that the outer query can filter, join, or aggregate further:

# Rank quality grades by their median-like percentile
pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT *
    FROM (
        SELECT
            quality_grade,
            COUNT(*) AS n,
            ROUND(AVG(above_ground_sqft), 0) AS avg_sqft
        FROM buildings
        WHERE quality_grade IS NOT NULL
        GROUP BY quality_grade
    ) AS grade_stats
    WHERE n >= 50
    ORDER BY avg_sqft DESC;
""", conn)

title: 14  Extending Data with SQL
text:
14.7 Part 2 Exercises






NoteExercise 3: Scalar subquery — newest buildings





Write a query that finds all buildings built in the most recent year in the database (without hardcoding the year). How many are there?






NoteSolution






pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT COUNT(*) AS n_newest,
           MAX(year_built) AS year
    FROM buildings
    WHERE year_built = (SELECT MAX(year_built) FROM buildings);
""", conn)













NoteExercise 4: WHERE NOT IN





Write a query that finds parcels (strap values) that have only non-residential building sections. Hint: find all parcels that appear in residential rows, then select parcels NOT IN that set.






NoteSolution






pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT DISTINCT strap
    FROM buildings
    WHERE strap NOT IN (
        SELECT DISTINCT strap
        FROM buildings
        WHERE bldg_class = 'R'
    )
    LIMIT 10;
""", conn)

title: 14  Extending Data with SQL
text:
14.8 Part 3: Common Table Expressions (CTEs)
A CTE is a named temporary result set defined at the top of a query with WITH ... AS. It makes complex queries readable by breaking them into named, sequential steps — the SQL equivalent of assigning intermediate DataFrames to variables in pandas.
The key distinction between a CTE and a subquery is organizational, not functional: both compute the same result, but a CTE gives the intermediate result a name. Use a subquery when the computation is simple and used once. Use a CTE when the intermediate result is complex, used more than once, or when naming it would make the overall query easier to read and audit.

14.8.1 The WITH … AS pattern
Compare these two approaches to the same question — “which quality grades have above-average square footage?”:
-- Subquery style (hard to read at depth)
SELECT * FROM (
    SELECT quality_grade, AVG(above_ground_sqft) AS avg_sqft
    FROM buildings GROUP BY quality_grade
) WHERE avg_sqft > (SELECT AVG(above_ground_sqft) FROM buildings);

-- CTE style (name the intermediate result)
WITH grade_stats AS (
    SELECT quality_grade, AVG(above_ground_sqft) AS avg_sqft
    FROM buildings
    GROUP BY quality_grade
)
SELECT *
FROM grade_stats
WHERE avg_sqft > (SELECT AVG(above_ground_sqft) FROM buildings);
Both produce the same result. The CTE version is easier to read, debug, and extend.


14.8.2 Single CTE

pd.read_sql_query("""
    WITH grade_stats AS (
        SELECT
            quality_grade,
            COUNT(*) AS n,
            ROUND(AVG(above_ground_sqft), 0) AS avg_sqft,
            ROUND(AVG(year_built), 0) AS avg_year
        FROM buildings
        WHERE quality_grade IS NOT NULL
        GROUP BY quality_grade
    )
    SELECT *
    FROM grade_stats
    WHERE n >= 50
    ORDER BY avg_sqft DESC;
""", conn)



14.8.3 Chained CTEs: multi-step analytical pipeline
You can define multiple CTEs separated by commas, each referencing the ones defined before it:

pd.read_sql_query("""
    WITH era_stats AS (
        SELECT
            (year_built / 10) * 10 AS decade,
            COUNT(*) AS n,
            ROUND(AVG(above_ground_sqft), 0) AS avg_sqft
        FROM buildings
        WHERE year_built IS NOT NULL
        GROUP BY decade
    ),
    overall AS (
        SELECT ROUND(AVG(above_ground_sqft), 0) AS grand_avg
        FROM buildings
        WHERE year_built IS NOT NULL
    )
    SELECT
        e.decade,
        e.n,
        e.avg_sqft,
        o.grand_avg,
        ROUND(e.avg_sqft - o.grand_avg, 0) AS diff_from_avg
    FROM era_stats e, overall o
    ORDER BY e.decade;
""", conn)

This pattern — define intermediate results as named CTEs, then combine them in a final SELECT — is the standard way to build complex SQL analyses. It makes every step visible and auditable.

title: 14  Extending Data with SQL
text:
14.9 Part 3 Exercises






NoteExercise 5: CTE — quality grade leaderboard





Write a CTE-based query that ranks quality grades by total square footage. Include the count of buildings, total square footage, and average square footage per building.






NoteSolution






pd.read_sql_query("""
    WITH grade_totals AS (
        SELECT
            quality_grade,
            COUNT(*) AS n_buildings,
            SUM(above_ground_sqft) AS total_sqft,
            ROUND(AVG(above_ground_sqft), 0) AS avg_sqft
        FROM buildings
        WHERE quality_grade IS NOT NULL AND above_ground_sqft IS NOT NULL
        GROUP BY quality_grade
    )
    SELECT *
    FROM grade_totals
    ORDER BY total_sqft DESC;
""", conn)













NoteExercise 6: Chained CTEs — era comparison





Write a query with two CTEs: one for pre-2000 buildings and one for 2000-and-later buildings. Compare the average square footage, count, and most common quality grade between the two eras.






NoteSolution






pd.read_sql_query("""
    WITH pre_2000 AS (
        SELECT
            'Pre-2000' AS era,
            COUNT(*) AS n,
            ROUND(AVG(above_ground_sqft), 0) AS avg_sqft
        FROM buildings
        WHERE year_built < 2000 AND year_built IS NOT NULL
    ),
    post_2000 AS (
        SELECT
            '2000+' AS era,
            COUNT(*) AS n,
            ROUND(AVG(above_ground_sqft), 0) AS avg_sqft
        FROM buildings
        WHERE year_built >= 2000 AND year_built IS NOT NULL
    )
    SELECT * FROM pre_2000
    UNION ALL
    SELECT * FROM post_2000;
""", conn)

text:

SQL

df.groupby('col')

title: 14  Extending Data with SQL
text:
14.11 Quick Reference

14.11.1 Subquery patterns

Scalar: returns one value → use in WHERE, SELECT, or HAVING
Column: returns one column → use with IN or NOT IN
Derived table: returns a table → use in FROM clause



14.11.2 CTE pattern
WITH step1 AS (
    SELECT ... FROM ... GROUP BY ...
),
step2 AS (
    SELECT ... FROM step1 WHERE ...
)
SELECT ... FROM step2;
CTEs are evaluated once, named for clarity, and discarded after the query completes. Use them whenever a query needs more than one level of aggregation or filtering.

title: 14  Extending Data with SQL
text:
14.12 Common Issues in This Chapter






WarningError: misuse of aggregate or non-aggregated columns in SELECT



What it means: Every column in a SELECT with GROUP BY must either appear in GROUP BY or be wrapped in an aggregate function. SQLite enforces this loosely but will return unpredictable values for non-aggregated columns.
How to fix it: Add the missing column to GROUP BY, or wrap it in MIN(), MAX(), or another aggregate. If you want a per-group value that is not an aggregate (like a representative name), think about whether you actually want GROUP BY or whether a window function / CTE is more appropriate.








WarningAVG() silently excludes NULL rows



What it means: AVG(col) returns the mean of non-NULL values. If many rows have NULL in that column, your average is over a smaller group than you expected.
How to fix it: Compare COUNT(*) with COUNT(col) to see the completeness rate. If you want to treat NULL as zero, use AVG(COALESCE(col, 0)). Document the choice in your Known Issues Log either way.








WarningA subquery returns more than one row where a scalar is expected



What it means: You used a subquery in a position that expects a single value (WHERE x > (SELECT ...)), but the subquery returned multiple rows.
How to fix it: Add an aggregate (AVG, MAX, MIN) to the inner query. Or switch to IN (SELECT ...) if you meant “any of these values.”








Warningstrftime returns NULL



What it means: The input string is not in ISO format (YYYY-MM-DD), or the month is stored as 3 instead of 03.
How to fix it: Use printf('%02d', month) to zero-pad single-digit months. Double-check the reconstructed string with a SELECT before applying strftime.

title: 14  Extending Data with SQL
text:
14.13 Key Takeaways

GROUP BY + aggregates is the SQL equivalent of df.groupby().agg(). Same idea, different syntax.
WHERE filters rows before grouping; HAVING filters groups after aggregation. Getting this order right is half of reading a GROUP BY query correctly.
CASE WHEN inside aggregates is the most powerful pattern in this chapter — it unlocks cross-tabulation, conditional counting, and every other “if-then” summary.
Prefer CTEs over nested subqueries when a query has more than one level of aggregation. They are easier to read, debug, and modify.
SQL queries are declarative: you describe the result, not the procedure. The database engine figures out the execution plan.

text:
